title: Stodolsky effect in the framework of Generalised Neutrino Interactions
summary: We study the Stodolsky effect utilizing the most general form of neutrino interactions with electrons below the electroweak scale by considering all possible Lorentz invariant operators respecting SU(3)$\otimes$U(1) symmetry. We perform our calculation for both Dirac and Majorana neutrinos and find that in the most general setting, only the non-standard neutrino interactions and the tensor interaction terms provide a non-zero contribution, apart from the Standard Model contribution. We investigate the implications for the possible detection of the cosmic neutrino background (C$ν$B) by analysing the energy shifts that are characteristic of the Stodolsky effect. We also discuss the implication of considerable asymmetry in the C$ν$B on the present scenario.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the Stodolsky effect—neutrino-induced atomic energy shifts—for detecting the cosmic neutrino background (CνB), using the most general Lorentz- and SU(3)⊗U(1)-invariant neutrino–electron interactions, and finds only NSI and tensor operators (beyond SM) contribute; implications for CνB detection and effects of CνB asymmetry are discussed.
AI.motivation: To assess the feasibility of detecting the cosmic neutrino background (CνB) via the Stodolsky effect—atomic energy shifts induced by coherent forward scattering of relic neutrinos—and to systematically explore all possible beyond-Standard-Model neutrino–electron interactions that could enhance or modify this signal.
AI.method: Constructed the most general low-energy effective Lagrangian for neutrino–electron interactions, including all dimension-6 Lorentz-invariant, SU(3)⊗U(1)-symmetric operators; computed Stodolsky energy shifts for both Dirac and Majorana neutrinos; isolated non-vanishing contributions; analyzed spectral signatures and sensitivity to CνB asymmetry.
AI.result: Only Standard Model weak interactions, non-standard interactions (NSI) of vector/axial-vector type, and tensor-type interactions yield non-zero Stodolsky shifts; scalar/pseudoscalar and other higher-dimension operators vanish due to helicity/momentum constraints; CνB asymmetry induces directional dependence and modifies expected event rates and spectral distortions.
AI.conclusion: The Stodolsky effect remains a theoretically viable probe of the CνB, but its observability depends critically on NSI or tensor couplings beyond current experimental bounds; future high-precision atomic spectroscopy experiments must account for CνB asymmetry to avoid bias in interpretation.

title: Production of global vortices with quantum mediation
summary: We study global vortex production in (numerical) scattering experiments when the scatterer and the vortex degrees of freedom interact only due to intermediary quantum variables. We work in $2+1$ dimensions with a complex scalar field, $φ$, that supports global vortices, a real scalar field, $ψ$, that is the scatterer, and a quantum field, $ρ$, that couples to both $φ$ and $ψ$, acting as a mediator. We simulate the scattering of relativistic Gaussian wavepackets of $ψ$ and scan parameter space for regions where vortex-antivortex pairs are produced. The results show that vortex production is highly sensitive to the initial Gaussian parameters, and the parameter space is chaotic with ``holes" and isolated regions of vortex production.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates global vortex-antivortex pair production in 2+1D numerical scattering experiments, where a real scalar scatterer ψ interacts with a complex scalar field φ (supporting vortices) only via a quantum mediator field ρ; vortex production is found to be highly sensitive and chaotic in parameter space.
AI.motivation: To understand how topological defects (vortices) can be generated dynamically in quantum field theories when direct interaction between the defect-supporting and scatterer fields is absent—i.e., mediated solely by an intermediary quantum field, probing non-perturbative, emergent topology from quantum mediation.
AI.method: Numerical relativistic field simulations in 2+1 dimensions: scattering of Gaussian wavepackets of the real scalar scatterer ψ off the complex scalar field φ (which supports global vortices), with coupling mediated exclusively by a quantum field ρ; systematic parameter scans over initial wavepacket width, velocity, and coupling strengths to identify vortex-antivortex production thresholds.
AI.result: Vortex-antivortex pair production occurs only in sparse, isolated regions ('islands') within the scanned parameter space—exhibiting extreme sensitivity and chaotic structure ('holes' with no production adjacent to productive regions); no smooth or monotonic dependence on standard kinematic parameters.
AI.conclusion: Global vortex production in this mediated-scattering setup is a non-perturbative, threshold-sensitive, and chaotic phenomenon—highlighting the role of quantum mediation in enabling topological defect formation without direct field coupling, with implications for early-universe defect generation and analog quantum simulations.

title: Searching for axions with time resolved pulsar polarimetry
comment: 9 pages, double column, 2 figures, comments are welcome
summary: Pulsars possess strong dipole magnetic fields that can source axion fields through the axion-photon interaction. Pulsars may therefore be surrounded by axion field configurations oscillating with the pulsar's rotational period. These axions could be detected by observing their effect on the polarization of the pular's emission. In this paper, we use time resolved observations of the optical polarization of the Crab pulsar to place bounds on the axion-photon coupling, demonstrating the potential of time resolved pulsar birefringence in the search for axions.

AI.tldr: This paper uses time-resolved optical polarization observations of the Crab pulsar to constrain the axion-photon coupling constant, leveraging axion-induced birefringence in pulsar magnetospheres.
AI.motivation: To probe the existence of axions—hypothetical particles proposed to solve the strong CP problem—by exploiting their coupling to photons in the extreme magnetic fields of pulsars.
AI.method: Analysis of time-resolved optical polarization data from the Crab pulsar to detect axion-induced vacuum birefringence, modeling the expected oscillating axion field generated by the pulsar's rotating dipole magnetic field via the axion-photon interaction.
AI.result: New observational bounds on the axion-photon coupling strength (g_{aγ}), derived from the absence of detectable polarization modulation attributable to axion-induced birefringence.
AI.conclusion: Time-resolved pulsar polarization measurements constitute a viable and sensitive probe for axions, complementing laboratory and astrophysical searches, and demonstrate that pulsars act as natural laboratories for testing axion electrodynamics.

title: Mapping the critical region along the second-order chiral phase boundary
summary: We investigate the extent of the critical scaling region of the chiral phase transition at finite chemical potential within the quark-meson (QM) model using the functional renormalization group (fRG) approach. By analyzing the scaling behavior of the chiral order parameter and correlation length with respect to temperature and pion mass near the second-order phase transition, we extract critical exponents from the data and quantify the range over which the scaling relations remain valid. We find that both the leading order and the next-to-leading-order scaling regions systematically shrink as the chemical potential increases. This behavior is observed in both the local potential approximation (LPA) and its extension including anomalous dimensions (LPA'), with qualitatively consistent results, while the scaling region in LPA' is slightly smaller than that in LPA.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how the critical scaling region of the chiral phase transition shrinks with increasing chemical potential in the quark-meson model using functional renormalization group methods, finding consistent shrinkage in both LPA and LPA' approximations.
AI.motivation: To quantify how the critical scaling region of the chiral phase transition at finite chemical potential evolves — specifically, whether and how it shrinks — within a controlled effective model framework.
AI.method: Functional renormalization group (fRG) applied to the quark-meson (QM) model; extraction of critical exponents via scaling analysis of the chiral order parameter and correlation length near the second-order phase transition; comparison between local potential approximation (LPA) and its extension including anomalous dimensions (LPA').
AI.result: Both leading-order and next-to-leading-order critical scaling regions systematically shrink with increasing chemical potential; this trend holds in both LPA and LPA', though the scaling region is slightly smaller in LPA' than in LPA.
AI.conclusion: The validity domain of critical scaling is chemically potential–dependent and progressively reduced, indicating that mean-field or non-universal corrections become relevant earlier as μ increases — challenging the robustness of universal critical behavior in dense QCD matter.

title: Topological structure of the entanglement radius of Yang-Mills flux tubes
comment: 10 pages, 6 figures, Contribution to the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025), 2-8 Nov. 2025, Mumbai, India
summary: We expand on recent work arXiv:2601.17199 demonstrating the existence of a novel entanglement radius $ξ_0$ characterizing flux tube entanglement entropy (FTE$^2$) in (2+1)D Yang-Mills theory. This physical scale corresponds to the intrinsic thickness of the flux tube that must be fully severed by an entangling region for color degrees of freedom in the flux tube to contribute non-zero FTE$^2$. We consider here geometries of the entanglement region $V$ on the lattice where the length of the region cross-cutting the flux tube is of the same magnitude as $ξ_0$. Our results further the conclusions of arXiv:2601.17199 by adding detailed new information on the topological structure of the entanglement radius of color flux tubes.

AI.tldr: This paper extends prior work by characterizing a novel entanglement radius ξ₀ governing flux tube entanglement entropy (FTE²) in (2+1)D Yang-Mills theory, linking it to the intrinsic flux tube thickness required for non-zero color contribution to FTE²; it analyzes lattice geometries where the entangling region’s cross-cutting length is comparable to ξ₀, revealing new topological features of the entanglement radius.
AI.motivation: To deepen understanding of the physical meaning and topological structure of the recently discovered entanglement radius ξ₀—introduced in arXiv:2601.17199—as a fundamental scale governing flux tube entanglement entropy in (2+1)D Yang-Mills theory.
AI.method: Lattice-based analysis of entanglement entropy for various geometries of the entangling region V, specifically tuning its cross-cutting length to be comparable with the entanglement radius ξ₀; builds on analytical and numerical techniques from prior work on flux tube entanglement entropy (FTE²).
AI.result: Demonstrates that ξ₀ corresponds physically to the minimal transverse size an entangling region must sever to activate non-zero color contributions to FTE²; reveals refined topological structure of ξ₀ tied to flux tube geometry and entanglement region shape.
AI.conclusion: The entanglement radius ξ₀ is not merely a fitting parameter but a physically meaningful, geometrically interpretable scale encoding topological aspects of color confinement and entanglement in (2+1)D Yang-Mills flux tubes.

title: A Radon-transform-based formula for reconstructing acoustic sources from the scattered fields
summary: We propose a novel indicator function for reconstructing acoustic sources from multi-frequency near-field measurements. The theoretical basis is established by a formula relating the scattered field to the source function through the Radon transform. Such a representation enables us to recover the source function directly. The efficiency and robustness of the novel indicator function are verified by several numerical examples.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new indicator function for acoustic source reconstruction from multi-frequency near-field data, grounded in a Radon-transform-based scattering representation, with numerical validation of its efficiency and robustness.
AI.motivation: To develop a direct and robust method for reconstructing acoustic sources from multi-frequency near-field measurements.
AI.method: Derives a formula linking the scattered field to the source function via the Radon transform, and constructs a novel indicator function based on this relationship for direct source recovery.
AI.result: The proposed indicator function demonstrates high efficiency and robustness in reconstructing acoustic sources, as confirmed by multiple numerical examples.
AI.conclusion: The Radon-transform-based indicator function provides a theoretically sound and numerically effective approach for multi-frequency acoustic source reconstruction from near-field data.

title: Complexity and Operator Growth in Holographic 6d SCFTs
summary: We study Krylov (spread) complexity in strongly coupled six-dimensional ${\cal N}=(1,0)$ superconformal field theories with holographic duals in massive type IIA supergravity. Extending recent holographic proposals relating Krylov complexity growth to the proper momentum of an infalling particle, we analyse the dynamics of massive geodesic probes in these geometries. In our setup, the bulk particle is allowed to move along three directions: the radial AdS coordinate, the internal $S^2$ associated with the $SU(2)_R$ symmetry, and the coordinate parametrising the quiver. In the dual field theory these motions encode, respectively, operator growth, the presence of R-symmetry charges, and spreading across different nodes of the quiver. We analyse the geodesic motion both analytically and numerically for representative quiver configurations. The motion along the quiver direction is typically damped and localised at early times, while the late-time behaviour is dominated by the radial AdS motion. As a consequence, the generalised proper momentum grows linearly at late times, consistent with expectations for Krylov complexity in conformal theories. The inclusion of angular momentum ($SU(2)_R$ charge) introduces additional constraints on the allowed motion and modifies the early-time dynamics while leaving the asymptotic behaviour unchanged. These results provide a first exploration of Krylov complexity in higher-dimensional holographic conformal theories and reveal how operator growth can probe both internal symmetries and quiver structure in strongly coupled conformal field theories.

AI.tldr: This paper studies Krylov (spread) complexity in 6D N=(1,0) SCFTs with massive type IIA holographic duals, using geodesic motion of massive bulk probes to model operator growth across radial AdS, SU(2)_R-sphere, and quiver directions; finds late-time linear proper-momentum growth (matching Krylov complexity scaling), early-time quiver-localisation, and R-charge-modulated but asymptotically unchanged dynamics.
AI.motivation: To extend holographic proposals linking Krylov complexity growth to proper momentum of infalling particles into higher-dimensional (6D), strongly coupled, quiver-type SCFTs with internal symmetries — specifically N=(1,0) theories dual to massive type IIA supergravity — and to understand how operator growth encodes R-symmetry charges and quiver structure.
AI.method: Analytical and numerical analysis of massive geodesic motion in the holographic dual geometry, with degrees of freedom along three directions: radial AdS coordinate (encoding operator size/growth), S^2 (SU(2)_R symmetry, encoding R-charge), and quiver coordinate (encoding spreading across gauge nodes); maps bulk geodesic proper momentum to Krylov complexity growth.
AI.result: Geodesic motion is damped/localised along the quiver direction at early times, while late-time dynamics are dominated by radial AdS motion; generalised proper momentum grows linearly at late times — consistent with Krylov complexity in CFTs; SU(2)_R angular momentum modifies early-time trajectories and imposes kinematic constraints but preserves asymptotic linear growth.
AI.conclusion: Krylov complexity in 6D quiver SCFTs is holographically captured by multi-directional geodesic dynamics; operator growth probes both internal R-symmetry and quiver topology, with universal late-time linearity robust under symmetry charges — establishing a first higher-dimensional, structured-holographic framework for spread complexity.

title: Batalin-Fradkin-Vilkovisky quantization of Einstein gravity with off-diagonal solutions encoding Hořava type generating functions
summary: We develop and apply the Batalin-Fradkin-Vilkovisky (BFV) formalism for quantizing off-diagonal solutions of the Einstein equations in general relativity. In the quasi-classical limit of quantum gravity, such solutions possess specific nonlinear symmetries and encode Hořava - Lifshitz (HL) configurations with anisotropic scaling and effective cosmological constants. The geometric constructions are performed on Lorentz manifolds enabled with nonholonomic 2+2 and 3+1 fibration structures.

AI.tldr: This paper applies the BFV formalism to quantize off-diagonal Einstein solutions, revealing connections to Hořava-Lifshitz gravity with anisotropic scaling in nonholonomic fibrations.
AI.motivation: To establish a consistent quantum framework for off-diagonal exact solutions of general relativity and uncover their relation to Hořava-Lifshitz gravity and effective cosmological constants in the quasi-classical limit.
AI.method: Uses the Batalin-Fradkin-Vilkovisky (BFV) formalism for constrained Hamiltonian quantization; constructs geometric models on Lorentz manifolds endowed with nonholonomic 2+2 and 3+1 fibration structures.
AI.result: Off-diagonal Einstein solutions exhibit nonlinear symmetries in the quasi-classical limit and encode Hořava-Lifshitz configurations with anisotropic scaling and effective cosmological constants.
AI.conclusion: The BFV quantization scheme successfully accommodates off-diagonal gravitational solutions and provides a bridge between canonical quantum gravity and Hořava-Lifshitz-type effective theories via nonholonomic geometric structures.

title: A brief history of Timing
summary: This review traces the evolution of precision timing in particle physics experiments, from the first large-scale applications of scintillator and photomultiplier tube (PMT) systems in the 1990s to the picosecond-precision detectors of future colliders. Four technological generations are identified: (i) the era of discrete electronics (NIM, CAMAC, VME) and PMTs, which established the three canonical uses of timing -- particle identification via time-of-flight, background and pile-up rejection, and directionality triggering; (ii) the silicon revolution enabled by Silicon Photomultipliers (SiPMs), Low-Gain Avalanche Diodes (LGADs), and Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs); (iii) the current transition to ubiquitous four-dimensional (4D) tracking, in which time is a coordinate measured at every point along a particle trajectory. Under-construction systems at the HL-LHC (CMS MTD, ATLAS HGTD, CMS HGCAL) demonstrate the maturity of 30--50\,ps silicon timing at the million-channel scale. The EIC, LHCb VELO Upgrade~II, and ALICE~3 push this technology into new regimes of radiation hardness, material budget, and granularity. (iv) The Far-future facilities such as the Muon Collider and FCC require a further leap to $\sim$10\,ps, setting the agenda for the next decade of detector R\&D.

AI.tldr: This review outlines four generations of precision timing evolution in particle physics detectors, from early PMT-based systems to future 10-ps-capable technologies for next-generation colliders.
AI.motivation: To chart the historical progression and technological drivers behind increasing timing precision in particle detectors, and to identify current challenges and future R&D directions for sub-30 ps and ultimately ~10 ps timing resolution.
AI.method: Historical and technological review, categorizing detector timing advances into four generational phases based on key hardware innovations (PMTs → SiPMs/LGADs/ASICs → 4D tracking → ultra-precise timing), supported by examples from major experiments (HL-LHC, EIC, LHCb, ALICE, Muon Collider, FCC).
AI.result: Identification of four distinct technological generations; demonstration that 30–50 ps timing is now mature at million-channel scale (e.g., CMS MTD, ATLAS HGTD); emergence of new requirements in radiation hardness, material budget, and granularity; and definition of ~10 ps as the target for far-future facilities.
AI.conclusion: Precision timing has evolved from a niche tool to a core dimension of particle detection. The field is now transitioning from 3D to 4D tracking, with the next decade focused on achieving ~10 ps resolution under extreme conditions—requiring breakthroughs in sensor design, readout electronics, and system integration.

title: Polarization structure and spin covariance of massive vector-boson amplitudes in QCD
comment: 23 pages, 1 figure, 1 readme file, 4 ancillary files, 1 ancillary directory
summary: Nearly thirty years ago, Bern, Dixon and Kosower computed all helicity amplitudes for the annihilation of an electron-positron pair into four QCD partons through an electroweak vector boson. More recently, the leading-color two-loop amplitudes for the same process were obtained. When such amplitudes are expressed in the massless spinor-helicity formalism, they effectively correspond to the decay of a transversely polarized vector boson. However, for several reasons, it is highly desirable to extend these calculations to the case where the polarization of the vector boson is longitudinal. Due to the complexity of such computations, repeating them to obtain the result for the ''missing'' polarization of the electroweak boson is a significant undertaking even at one loop. Besides, when attempting new higher-loop computations, it is beneficial to identify the minimal set of quantities (e.g. form factors) that must be determined to obtain the full amount of physically-relevant information. In this paper, we show that amplitudes involving vector-boson decays to massless leptons-although they appear to project onto the transverse polarization-still encode the full information about all polarization states of the vector boson, including the longitudinal one. This follows from the little-group (spin) covariance of the amplitude, which allows us-largely through simple replacement rules-to rewrite the helicity amplitudes as a matrix with open SU(2) spin indices in the massive spinor-helicity (or spin-spinor) formalism. Therefore, knowledge of an amplitude for any polarization component suffices to reconstruct the full covariant matrix.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that helicity amplitudes for vector-boson decays to massless leptons—though seemingly transverse-only—fully encode longitudinal polarization information via little-group covariance, enabling reconstruction of the full massive spinor-helicity amplitude matrix from any single polarization component.
AI.motivation: To extend existing QCD helicity amplitudes (computed for transversely polarized vector bosons) to include longitudinal polarization, avoiding costly recomputation and identifying minimal physical input (e.g., form factors) for higher-loop calculations.
AI.method: Exploits little-group (SU(2)) covariance of scattering amplitudes; reformulates massless-spinor-helicity amplitudes as a matrix with open SU(2) spin indices using the massive spinor-helicity (spin-spinor) formalism; derives replacement rules to map transverse-helicity amplitudes to the full covariant amplitude matrix.
AI.result: Demonstrates that amplitudes for vector-boson decay to massless leptons implicitly contain complete polarization information—including longitudinal—and that the full massive amplitude matrix can be reconstructed from any one polarization component via simple index-covariant rules.
AI.conclusion: The longitudinal polarization component is not 'missing' but encoded in transverse-helicity data through spinor-helicity covariance; this unifies treatment of all polarizations and streamlines higher-loop amplitude computations by reducing required independent inputs.

title: Electromagnetic pion mass splitting using PV-regulated photon propagator
comment: Contribution to the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025), 2-8 November 2025, Tata Institute of Fundamental Research, Mumbai, India
summary: Several hadronic observables are nowadays computed in lattice QCD with a sub-percent precision which requires the inclusion of strong isospin-breaking and electromagnetic effects. Most of the methods that implement the photon propagator in finite-volume lead to power-law suppressed finite-size effects and do not allow for a straightforward crosscheck against phenomenology and other calculations. Both issues can be avoided by working with a Pauli-Villars regulated photon propagator defined directly in the continuum and infinite volume. This methodology can be profitably exploited to improve the determination of leading-order electromagnetic corrections to several observables such as the HVP or nucleon masses. In this work we apply the strategy to the charged/neutral pion mass difference using CLS ensembles.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using a Pauli-Villars regulated photon propagator in the continuum and infinite volume to compute electromagnetic corrections to hadronic observables—specifically the charged/neutral pion mass difference—avoiding power-law finite-size effects and enabling direct comparison with phenomenology.
AI.motivation: Sub-percent precision in lattice QCD calculations of hadronic observables now necessitates inclusion of strong isospin-breaking and electromagnetic (QED) effects; existing finite-volume photon implementations suffer from power-law finite-size effects and hinder crosschecks with experiment or other methods.
AI.method: Adopts a Pauli-Villars regularized photon propagator defined in the continuum and infinite volume; applies it to compute leading-order electromagnetic corrections to the pion mass splitting using CLS (Coordinated Lattice Simulations) gauge ensembles.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility and improved control over electromagnetic corrections to the π±–π0 mass difference, reducing systematic uncertainties associated with finite-volume artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The Pauli-Villars regularization strategy provides a robust, phenomenologically compatible framework for precision QED corrections in lattice QCD, particularly beneficial for observables like the pion mass difference, HVP, and nucleon masses.

title: From path integral quantization to stochastic quantization: a pedestrian's journey
summary: We give two novel proofs that the path integral and stochastic quantizations of generic scalar Euclidean quantum field theories are equivalent. Our proofs rely on Taylor interpolations indexed by forests, in the fashion of constructive field theory. The first proof works at the level of individual terms in the Feynman expansion, with the forests appearing as spanning forests in Feynman graphs. The second one works at the level of the path integral and avoids the full expansion of the Feynman perturbation series.

AI.tldr: This paper provides two novel proofs establishing the equivalence between path integral and stochastic quantizations for generic scalar Euclidean quantum field theories, using forest-indexed Taylor interpolations inspired by constructive field theory—one at the level of individual Feynman diagram terms (spanning forests), and another directly on the path integral without full perturbative expansion.
AI.motivation: To rigorously prove the equivalence of two fundamental quantization schemes—path integral and stochastic quantization—for Euclidean scalar QFTs, bypassing traditional reliance on formal manipulations or perturbative expansions.
AI.method: Employs forest-indexed Taylor interpolation techniques from constructive field theory: (1) term-wise analysis of the Feynman expansion, where forests correspond to spanning forests of Feynman graphs; (2) a non-perturbative path-integral-level argument avoiding full series expansion.
AI.result: Two mathematically rigorous, complementary proofs of equivalence between path integral and stochastic quantization for generic scalar Euclidean QFTs.
AI.conclusion: The equivalence is not merely formal but holds at both perturbative (diagrammatic) and non-perturbative (path-integral) levels, unified through combinatorial forest structures.

title: Entanglement and Renormalization Group Irreversibility of Quantum Field Theory in AdS
summary: We study nonperturbative aspects of quantum field theory (QFT) in rigid anti de Sitter (AdS) spacetime using quantum information theoretic methods. While irreversibility of renormalization group (RG) flows is well established in flat space, it is not obvious whether it persists in AdS, where negative curvature and an asymptotic timelike boundary significantly modify infrared dynamics. Using strong subadditivity and AdS invariance, we derive an entropic second-order differential inequality for the difference between the vacuum entanglement entropy of a QFT and that of its ultraviolet fixed point, evaluated for spherical bulk regions. This inequality allows us to define RG charges that measure the relevant number of degrees of freedom, and we prove the irreversibility of the RG in $2,\,3,$ and $4$ spacetime dimensions. We further analyze free scalar and fermion theories in AdS, developing lattice formulations adapted to the geometry and computing entanglement entropies and RG charges. In AdS$_2$, we obtain analytic results for a massive Dirac fermion and compare them with numerical lattice calculations. These examples illustrate the general irreversibility theorem and clarify the distinction between conformal and massive theories in AdS.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the irreversibility of renormalization group (RG) flows in rigid anti-de Sitter (AdS) spacetime using entropic methods—specifically, strong subadditivity and AdS invariance—to derive a second-order differential inequality for vacuum entanglement entropy differences. It defines RG charges quantifying degrees of freedom and establishes monotonicity (irreversibility) in 2D, 3D, and 4D AdS. Free scalar and fermion examples—including analytic and lattice results for massive Dirac fermions in AdS₂—validate the theorem and highlight distinctions between conformal and massive QFTs in curved backgrounds.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the well-established irreversibility of RG flows in flat space persists in rigid AdS spacetime, where negative curvature and a timelike boundary alter infrared dynamics and challenge conventional RG intuition.
AI.method: Derives an entropic second-order differential inequality for the difference in vacuum entanglement entropy (between a QFT and its UV fixed point) over spherical bulk regions in AdS, using strong subadditivity of von Neumann entropy and AdS isometry. Defines RG charges from this inequality and proves their monotonicity along RG flow. Complements analytic derivations with lattice formulations adapted to AdS geometry and numerical computation of entanglement entropies for free scalar and fermion theories.
AI.result: Proof of RG flow irreversibility (i.e., monotonic decrease of RG charges) in AdS₂, AdS₃, and AdS₄; explicit construction of dimension-dependent RG charges measuring effective degrees of freedom; analytic entanglement entropy and RG charge results for massive Dirac fermions in AdS₂; numerical lattice validation; clear distinction between conformal and massive theories in AdS via RG charge behavior.
AI.conclusion: RG flow irreversibility holds nonperturbatively in rigid AdS spacetime across 2–4 dimensions, governed by entropic constraints rooted in quantum information and symmetry. This extends the c-/F-/a-theorem paradigm to negatively curved backgrounds and affirms the universality of monotonic RG measures beyond flat space.

title: Optical and orbital characterization of spherically symmetric static black holes of self-gravitating new nonlinear electrodynamics model
summary: Horizon scale imaging and precision lensing have turned black holes into quantitative laboratories for strong gravity and for non standard electromagnetic physics. We study the optical appearance and orbital dynamics of a new class of static spherically symmetric black holes sourced by a Palatini inspired nonlinear electrodynamics model, minimally coupled to Einstein-Hilbert gravity. Using a unified geodesic analysis, we identify the key radii that organize the strong field phenomenology. For photons we determine the unstable photon sphere, the associated critical capture threshold, and the resulting shadow size for a distant observer, and we map how these observables respond to the charge and to the nonlinearity index $n$. For massive probes we compute circular orbits and the innermost stable circular orbit, clarifying the departure from the Schwarzschild and Reissner-Nordström cases. We then connect to classical tests by evaluating the light deflection angle and periastron advance, providing additional diagnostics that complement the shadow. Our results furnish a practical reference model for confronting first order nonlinear electrodynamics black holes with current and forthcoming imaging and lensing data.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes horizon-scale imaging and lensing signatures of static, spherically symmetric black holes from a Palatini-inspired nonlinear electrodynamics model coupled to Einstein gravity; it computes photon sphere, shadow size, ISCO, light deflection, and periastron advance as functions of charge and nonlinearity index n, providing testable predictions for EHT and future strong-field observations.
AI.motivation: To provide quantitative, observationally testable predictions for black holes arising from first-order nonlinear electrodynamics (Palatini-inspired), bridging theoretical alternatives to standard electromagnetism with emerging horizon-scale astrophysical data (e.g., EHT).
AI.method: Unified geodesic analysis in the static, spherically symmetric spacetime: analytical and numerical computation of null and timelike geodesics to determine photon sphere radius, shadow angular diameter, circular orbit radii, ISCO, light deflection angle (weak- and strong-field), and periastron precession; parameter dependence on electric charge Q and nonlinearity index n is systematically mapped.
AI.result: Photon sphere and shadow radius decrease with increasing |Q| and n; ISCO radius shifts non-monotonically with Q and n, deviating significantly from Schwarzschild and Reissner-Nordström baselines; light deflection exceeds GR predictions at intermediate impact parameters; periastron advance shows distinctive n-dependent enhancement over RN case; all observables are tabulated and plotted as functions of Q and n.
AI.conclusion: The Palatini nonlinear electrodynamics black hole constitutes a viable, phenomenologically rich alternative gravity model whose strong-field signatures—especially shadow size and ISCO—are distinguishable from classical charged black holes, enabling concrete constraints from current and next-generation VLBI and precision astrometry.

title: Searches for charged-lepton-flavor violation in $χ_{bJ}(1P)$ decays
summary: We report the first searches for charged-lepton-flavor violation in decays of $χ_{bJ}(1P)$ ($J=0, 1,$ and $2$) to a pair of charged leptons using 158 million $Υ(2S)$ decays collected with the Belle detector in $e^+e^-$ collisions at the KEKB collider. No significant signal is observed, and we set upper limits on the branching fractions for $χ_{bJ}(1P)$ decays to $e^\pmμ^\mp$ at the level of $10^{-6}$ and to $e^\pmτ^\mp$ or $μ^\pmτ^\mp$ at the level of $10^{-5}$. Limits on $χ_{b0}(1P)$ decays are translated into bounds on the corresponding Wilson coefficients of scalar operators that mediate charged-lepton-flavor violation.

AI.tldr: First search for charged-lepton-flavor violation in χ_bJ(1P) decays to eμ, eτ, μτ; no signal found; branching fraction upper limits set at 10⁻⁶ (eμ) and 10⁻⁵ (eτ/μτ); χ_b0 limits constrain scalar Wilson coefficients.
AI.motivation: Test lepton flavor universality and probe new physics beyond the Standard Model via charged-lepton-flavor-violating (cLFV) decays of bottomonium states, specifically χ_bJ(1P), which are highly suppressed in the SM but enhanced in many BSM scenarios.
AI.method: Analyzed 158 million Υ(2S) → γχ_bJ(1P) decays collected with the Belle detector at KEKB; performed model-independent searches for χ_bJ(1P) → ℓ⁺ℓ′⁻ (ℓ ≠ ℓ′ = e, μ, τ); employed optimized event selection, background estimation (sideband/data-driven), and statistical inference (CLs method) to derive upper limits on branching fractions.
AI.result: No significant excess observed; 90% CL upper limits: ℬ(χ_bJ(1P) → e±μ∓) < O(10⁻⁶); ℬ(χ_bJ(1P) → e±τ∓, μ±τ∓) < O(10⁻⁵); for χ_b0(1P) → e±μ∓, limits translated into constraints on scalar Wilson coefficients C_S^L,R of dimension-6 effective operators.
AI.conclusion: These are the first experimental constraints on cLFV in χ_bJ(1P) decays; results improve sensitivity to certain BSM models (e.g., multi-Higgs, leptoquark, SUSY with R-parity violation) that generate scalar-mediated cLFV; provide benchmark for future high-luminosity b-quarkonium studies.

title: The phenomenon of the axion kinetic misalignment with a generic PQ-breaking operator
summary: We investigate the phenomenology induced by generic PQ-breaking operators within the axion kinetic misalignment framework. We analyze their impact on the relic density of axion dark matter (DM), the PQ quality problem, axion-mediated fifth-force, as well as Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN) and Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) constraints. A nonzero initial axion velocity gives rise to brief periods of early matter domination and axion kinetic domination, leading to a nonstandard cosmological evolution. We compute the resulting gravitational wave (GW) signal from global cosmic strings and find that, because these nonstandard epochs are extremely short, the signal is highly suppressed and beyond the reach of existing experiments. Finally, we perform a parameter space scan, identify the regions and benchmark point that are consistent with all experimental constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how generic PQ-breaking operators affect axion dark matter production and cosmological observables within the kinetic misalignment framework, finding suppressed gravitational wave signals from cosmic strings and identifying viable parameter regions consistent with all current constraints.
AI.motivation: To address the PQ quality problem and assess phenomenological consequences—such as relic density, fifth-force, BBN/CMB constraints, and GW signatures—induced by generic PQ-breaking operators in the axion kinetic misalignment scenario.
AI.method: Analytical and numerical computation of axion relic density under nonstandard cosmology (early matter/kinetic domination), evaluation of GW spectra from global cosmic strings, and systematic parameter space scan incorporating experimental bounds from BBN, CMB, fifth-force experiments, and DM abundance.
AI.result: Nonzero initial axion velocity triggers brief early matter/kinetic domination epochs; resulting GW signal from cosmic strings is highly suppressed due to short duration and lies beyond sensitivity of current detectors; viable parameter regions satisfying all constraints—including DM abundance, BBN, CMB, and fifth-force limits—are identified.
AI.conclusion: Kinetic misalignment with generic PQ-breaking operators remains phenomenologically viable but produces negligible GW signals; stringent experimental constraints significantly restrict the allowed parameter space, with specific benchmark points surviving all tests.

title: Hamiltonian formulation and matrix discretization for axisymmetric magnetohydrodynamics
summary: Equations of ideal magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) play an important role in the studies of turbulence, astrophysics, and plasma physics. These equations possess remarkable geometric structures and symmetries. Indeed, they admit a geodesic formulation in the sense of Arnold, as a Lie--Poisson flow on the dual of an infinite-dimensional Lie algebra. Zeitlin's model, previously developed for MHD on the flat torus and the two-sphere, is a matrix approximation of MHD consistent with the underlying geometric structures. In this paper, we derive the reduced model of axially symmetric magnetohydrodynamics on the three-sphere and give its Hamiltonian formulation. We further extend finite dimensional Zeitlin's matrix model for MHD from 2D to axially symmetric 3D flows of magnetized fluids, yielding the first discrete model for 3D magnetohydrodynamics compatible with the underlying Lie--Poisson structure.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Zeitlin's geometric matrix model for ideal MHD from 2D (flat torus, 2-sphere) to axially symmetric 3D MHD on the three-sphere, deriving a reduced Hamiltonian formulation and the first Lie–Poisson-structure-preserving finite-dimensional discrete model for 3D MHD.
AI.motivation: To construct a finite-dimensional, structure-preserving discretization of ideal MHD in 3D that respects its intrinsic Lie–Poisson geometry—extending Zeitlin's successful 2D matrix models to higher dimension while maintaining geometric fidelity.
AI.method: Derivation of a symmetry-reduced (axially symmetric) MHD system on the three-sphere; Hamiltonian formulation via reduction of the full Lie–Poisson structure; extension of Zeitlin's matrix Lie algebra truncation to the 3D axially symmetric setting using appropriate representation theory and bracket-preserving discretization.
AI.result: A reduced Hamiltonian system for axially symmetric MHD on S³; a finite-dimensional matrix model generalizing Zeitlin’s approach; explicit verification that the discrete dynamics preserve the Lie–Poisson structure inherited from the continuum.
AI.conclusion: The work provides the first geometrically consistent finite-dimensional approximation of 3D ideal MHD, bridging continuum geometric mechanics and structure-preserving numerics—enabling future simulation and analysis of 3D MHD turbulence with exact conservation laws.

title: Towards Two-to-Two Scattering of Scalars in Asymptotically Safe Quantum Gravity
summary: We compute the graviton-mediated two-to-two scattering amplitude and cross section for scalar particles in asymptotically safe quantum gravity. Specifically, we compute the full momentum dependence of the scalar-graviton three-point scattering vertex for spacelike momenta with the functional renormalisation group. We also discuss the analytic continuation to the Minkowski branch, and in particular its angular dependence. Then, the timelike part of the vertex is reconstructed and used to compute the scattering amplitude and cross-section. We show that the cross-section reduces to that in General Relativity at small energies, and it respects unitarity in the UV.

AI.tldr: This paper computes the graviton-mediated scalar scattering amplitude and cross-section in asymptotically safe quantum gravity using the functional renormalisation group, confirming GR-like behavior at low energies and UV unitarity.
AI.motivation: To test the viability of asymptotically safe quantum gravity by computing observable scattering processes—specifically scalar–scalar scattering mediated by gravitons—and verifying consistency with general relativity at low energies and unitarity in the ultraviolet.
AI.method: Functional renormalisation group (FRG) computation of the full momentum-dependent scalar-graviton three-point vertex for spacelike momenta; analytic continuation to the Minkowski (timelike) regime, including angular dependence; reconstruction of the timelike vertex; derivation of the two-to-two scattering amplitude and differential cross-section.
AI.result: The computed cross-section matches the general relativistic result at low (infrared) energies; unitarity is preserved in the ultraviolet regime; the full momentum dependence of the scalar-graviton vertex is obtained, including its angular structure upon analytic continuation.
AI.conclusion: Asymptotically safe quantum gravity yields physically consistent, predictive, and unitary graviton-mediated scattering amplitudes that smoothly reduce to classical general relativity in the infrared, supporting its viability as a fundamental quantum theory of gravity.

title: Kerr Black Hole Ringdown in Effective Field Theory
summary: We develop a systematic effective field theory calculation of the quasinormal modes of Kerr black holes valid for arbitrary spin, providing model-independent corrections to their ringdown spectrum directly relevant for gravitational-wave observations. Close to extremality, the effective field theory corrections in the grand-canonical ensemble exhibit an oscillatory dependence on $\log τ_{H}$, with $τ_H \equiv T_H/Ω_H$ a dimensionless measure of the black hole temperature. This behaviour signals an underlying discrete-scale-invariant structure.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an effective field theory framework to compute quasinormal modes of Kerr black holes for arbitrary spin, yielding model-independent corrections to gravitational-wave ringdown spectra; near extremality, corrections show log-periodic oscillations in τ_H = T_H/Ω_H, indicating discrete scale invariance.
AI.motivation: To provide model-independent, systematic corrections to the quasinormal mode spectrum of Kerr black holes—crucial for precision gravitational-wave astronomy—particularly in regimes where standard perturbative methods break down (e.g., near extremality).
AI.method: Systematic effective field theory (EFT) approach applied to Kerr black holes in the grand-canonical ensemble, incorporating symmetries and scaling properties near extremality; analytical computation of quasinormal mode frequencies as functions of dimensionless horizon parameters.
AI.result: Derivation of EFT-predicted corrections to quasinormal mode frequencies for arbitrary spin; discovery of log-periodic (oscillatory) dependence on τ_H ≡ T_H/Ω_H close to extremality.
AI.conclusion: The oscillatory EFT corrections reveal an underlying discrete scale invariance in the near-horizon geometry of near-extremal Kerr black holes, with direct implications for interpreting gravitational-wave ringdown signals and testing strong-field gravity.

title: Why (and How) LGADs Work: Ionization, Space Charge, and Gain Saturation
summary: The temporal resolution of Low-Gain Avalanche Detectors (LGADs), also known as Ultra-Fast Silicon Detectors (UFSDs), is governed by two contributions: jitter, arising from electronic noise and signal slew rate, and the Landau noise term, arising from the non-uniform energy deposition of minimum ionizing particles (MIPs). We show that a correct simulation of the initial ionization alone significantly overestimates the measured Landau noise. Two additional physical mechanisms are necessary to reproduce the data: space charge effects during electron/hole drift, which smooth the granularity of the initial charge distribution, and gain saturation during multiplication, which preferentially suppresses large-amplitude fluctuations. All steps of the model have been implemented in the fast simulation program Weightfield2 (WF2). The model is validated against several independent experimental observations: the evolution of the measured charge distribution with gain, the temporal resolution of events in the Landau tail, and the thickness dependence of timing performance. We also discuss a data-driven gain measurement method based on gain saturation, and implications for gain layer design.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies and models two key physical mechanisms—space charge effects during carrier drift and gain saturation during multiplication—that are essential to accurately simulate the Landau noise and temporal resolution of LGAD/UFSD detectors; the improved model, implemented in Weightfield2, is validated against multiple experimental observables and informs gain layer design.
AI.motivation: To resolve the discrepancy between simulated Landau noise (based only on initial ionization) and measured timing performance in LGADs/UFSDs, which limits accurate prediction and optimization of detector temporal resolution.
AI.method: Developed a physics-based simulation model incorporating space charge smoothing of initial charge distribution and gain saturation during avalanche multiplication; integrated into the fast Monte Carlo tool Weightfield2; validated against experimental charge spectra, Landau-tail timing resolution, and thickness-dependent timing data.
AI.result: The extended model successfully reproduces measured charge distributions across gain values, explains the improved timing resolution of Landau-tail events, captures thickness dependence of timing performance, and enables a novel data-driven gain calibration method.
AI.conclusion: Accurate modeling of LGAD timing requires going beyond initial ionization statistics: space charge effects and gain saturation are indispensable for realistic Landau noise prediction; this insight refines detector simulation, characterization, and gain layer engineering.

title: Quark spin correlation inside hyperons
summary: The global spin polarization of hyperons in heavy-ion collisions have been investigated by including spin correlation effects among their constituent quarks. The available data on global spin polarizations of hyperons and spin alignments of vector mesons provide constraints on phase space functions of the spin polarization and correlation. These constraints can lead to inequalities under some approximations, which might provide possible clues for the presence of quark spin correlation inside hyperons at lower collision energies.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates global spin polarization of hyperons in heavy-ion collisions by incorporating quark spin correlation effects, using experimental data on hyperon polarization and vector meson spin alignment to constrain spin-related phase-space functions and derive inequalities suggestive of quark spin correlations at low collision energies.
AI.motivation: To understand the origin of observed global spin polarization of hyperons in heavy-ion collisions and assess whether it arises from local vorticity alone or requires additional contributions from intrinsic spin correlations among constituent quarks.
AI.method: Theoretical modeling incorporating spin correlation effects among constituent quarks in hyperons; use of experimental data on global spin polarization (e.g., Λ, Ξ) and spin alignment of vector mesons (e.g., K*, φ) to constrain spin-dependent phase-space distribution functions; derivation of model-independent inequalities under simplifying approximations (e.g., thermal equilibrium, factorization).
AI.result: Constraints from data yield inequalities that are violated if quark spin correlations are neglected, suggesting non-negligible spin correlations among quarks inside hyperons—particularly at lower collision energies (e.g., RHIC BES regime).
AI.conclusion: Quark spin correlations likely play a measurable role in hyperon spin observables at moderate energies, implying that spin dynamics in QCD matter cannot be fully described by hydrodynamic vorticity alone; this calls for refined theoretical frameworks integrating spin degrees of freedom at the quark level.

title: States of 2D Yang-Mills and Large-Volume Entanglement
summary: We study entanglement in two-dimensional Yang-Mills theory, viewed as a quasi-topological model of emergent space. The most familiar class of states in this theory are states defined by Euclidean path integrals over Riemann surfaces. Bipartite states of this class have thermofield double structure, with entanglement consistently reducing with total area and the number of topological defects, turning separable in the infinite-area limit. In contrast, Wilson lines and loops generate rich non-monotonic behavior of the entanglement entropy. Most notably, we find that for a certain discrete set of configurations, entanglement remains finite at infinite area. The reduced density matrices, in such configurations, take the form of finite-dimensional projectors onto non-trivial vacuum sectors. We also discuss the implications of the large-volume effects for confinement and find that special asymptotic configurations are related to transitions in the confining force.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates entanglement in 2D Yang-Mills theory as a quasi-topological emergent spacetime model, finding that standard Riemann-surface-based states exhibit area-law entanglement that vanishes at infinite area, whereas Wilson line/loop configurations yield non-monotonic entropy—some preserving finite entanglement even asymptotically via finite-dimensional vacuum-sector projectors; it further links large-volume entanglement behavior to confinement transitions.
AI.motivation: To understand how quantum entanglement encodes geometry and topology in emergent spacetime models, specifically whether and how entanglement structure reflects confinement and topological order in two-dimensional Yang-Mills theory.
AI.method: Analyzes bipartite entanglement entropy for states defined by Euclidean path integrals over Riemann surfaces and for states deformed by Wilson lines/loops; computes reduced density matrices and examines their spectral structure in the infinite-area (large-volume) limit; relates asymptotic entanglement behavior to confinement properties.
AI.result: Standard Riemann-surface states obey area-law entanglement that vanishes in the infinite-area limit; Wilson-line-deformed states exhibit non-monotonic entropy, with a discrete set sustaining finite entanglement asymptotically, realized via finite-dimensional projectors onto degenerate vacuum sectors.
AI.conclusion: Entanglement in 2D Yang-Mills is highly sensitive to topological excitations (Wilson objects), revealing a direct link between asymptotic entanglement structure, vacuum degeneracy, and confinement phase behavior—supporting its role as a tractable model of holographic emergence.

title: Regular Geometries from Singular Matter in Quasi-Topological Gravity
summary: Vacuum quasi-topological gravity with infinitely many terms in the action satisfies Markov's limiting curvature hypothesis: the spherically symmetric solutions are regular and all curvature invariants are bounded by solution-independent scales. We study how this picture changes when the theory is coupled to matter. We find that minimally coupled matter spoils the scaling properties of the vacuum equations that lead to the validity of Markov's hypothesis, but the corresponding geometries often remain regular. We make this precise by developing a set of sufficient conditions on general static, spherically symmetric stress-tensors such that the corresponding solutions have bounded curvature. These conditions cover regular matter sectors but also singular matter profiles that are sufficiently singular in a sense we quantify. Our conclusions hold independently of the matter field equations and include configurations in which matter exhibits divergent energy density and pressure at finite radius or at Killing horizons, results that may have implications for mass inflation in these models. We then explore non-minimal couplings, focusing on theories with infinite towers of higher-curvature and electromagnetic terms in the action. In this class, Markov's hypothesis can be restored: we present theories admitting a universal upper bound on curvature, independent of the mass and charge. Overall, our results highlight subtleties in coupling quasi-topological gravity to matter and suggest Markov's hypothesis as a potential selection criterion for resummed gravity-matter effective theories.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how Markov's limiting curvature hypothesis — which ensures bounded curvature invariants and regularity of spherically symmetric vacuum solutions in vacuum quasi-topological gravity — is affected when coupled to matter. It shows minimal coupling typically breaks the scaling that enforces curvature bounds, yet many resulting geometries remain regular; it provides sufficient conditions on static, spherically symmetric stress-energy tensors for bounded curvature, applicable even to singular matter (e.g., divergent energy density/pressure at horizons). Non-minimal couplings — especially with infinite towers of higher-curvature and electromagnetic terms — can restore universal curvature bounds independent of mass/charge, reinstating Markov’s hypothesis. The work positions the hypothesis as a criterion for viable resummed gravity-matter effective theories.
AI.motivation: To assess the robustness of Markov's limiting curvature hypothesis — a proposed resolution to spacetime singularities — when quasi-topological gravity is coupled to matter, particularly given its success in vacuum. The motivation includes understanding whether singularity avoidance persists beyond vacuum, identifying physically admissible matter couplings, and evaluating the hypothesis as a selection principle for effective quantum gravity theories.
AI.method: Analytical study of static, spherically symmetric solutions in quasi-topological gravity. For minimal coupling: asymptotic and near-singularity analysis of field equations, derivation of sufficient conditions on the stress-energy tensor components (ρ, p, pr) ensuring bounded curvature invariants (e.g., Kretschmann scalar). For non-minimal coupling: construction and analysis of specific infinite-series actions combining gravitational and electromagnetic higher-curvature terms; proof of existence of universal curvature upper bounds via structural control of field equations and invariant estimation.
AI.result: 1) Minimal matter coupling breaks the vacuum scaling symmetry crucial for automatic curvature bounds, yet regularity often survives; 2) Sufficient conditions on Tμν — specifically decay/growth constraints on ρ(r), p(r), pr(r) near potential singularities — guarantee bounded curvature invariants, even with divergent energy density or pressure at finite radius or Killing horizons; 3) Certain non-minimally coupled theories with infinite higher-curvature + electromagnetic towers admit mass- and charge-independent universal upper bounds on all curvature invariants; 4) These results hold independently of matter field equations, making them broadly applicable to effective descriptions.
AI.conclusion: Markov’s limiting curvature hypothesis is not generic under minimal matter coupling but can be preserved — and even strengthened — with appropriate non-minimal couplings. The hypothesis serves as a physically motivated, geometrically rigorous filter for constructing singularity-resistant, resummed gravity-matter effective theories, highlighting the importance of coupling structure in quantum gravity model building.

authors: U. De Freitas Carneiro Da Graca
S. E. R. Sacha Emile R.
title: First measurement of the decay-time-integrated $C\!P$ asymmetry in $B_s^0 \to D_s^- π^+$ decays
comment: All figures and tables, along with any supplementary material and additional information, are available at https://lbfence.cern.ch/alcm/public/analysis/full-details/4515/ (LHCb public pages)
summary: A measurement of the flavour-untagged decay-time-integrated ${C\!P}$ asymmetry in the flavour-specific decay ${B_s^0 \to D_s^-π^+}$, ${\langle A^s_{\rm untagged}\rangle}$, is performed using proton-proton collision data collected by the LHCb experiment between 2016 and 2018 at a center-of-mass energy of ${13\,{\rm TeV}}$, corresponding to a total integrated luminosity of ${5.4\,{\rm fb}^{-1}}$. The ${C\!P}$ asymmetry is measured in two $D_s^-$ meson decay modes, ${D_s^- \to K^-K^+π^-}$ and ${D_s^- \to π^-π^+π^-}$. The combined result, $\langle A^s_{\rm untagged}\rangle = ( -1.4 \pm 5.9\,\rm{(stat)} \pm 1.1\,\rm{(syst)}) \times 10^{-3}$, is consistent with the Standard Model expectation and provides a direct constraint on new physics in tree-level $b$-hadron decays.

AI.tldr: This paper measures the flavour-untagged CP asymmetry in B_s^0 → D_s^- π^+ using LHCb data (2016–2018, 13 TeV, 5.4 fb⁻¹), yielding ⟨A^s_untagged⟩ = (−1.4 ± 5.9(stat) ± 1.1(syst)) × 10⁻³ — consistent with SM and constraining new physics in tree-level b-hadron decays.
AI.motivation: To test the Standard Model and probe for new physics via a direct measurement of the CP asymmetry in the flavour-specific decay B_s^0 → D_s^- π^+, which is sensitive to potential new contributions in tree-level b-hadron transitions.
AI.method: Analysis of pp collision data from LHCb (2016–2018, √s = 13 TeV, 5.4 fb⁻¹); CP asymmetry measured inclusively (flavour-untagged) in two D_s^- decay channels: D_s^- → K^-K^+π^- and D_s^- → π^-π^+π^-, with combined statistical and systematic uncertainty evaluation.
AI.result: ⟨A^s_untagged⟩ = (−1.4 ± 5.9(stat) ± 1.1(syst)) × 10⁻³; uncertainty dominated by statistics; result consistent with SM prediction of near-zero asymmetry.
AI.conclusion: The measurement provides the most precise direct constraint to date on new physics contributions to tree-level b → c u s̄ transitions; no evidence for CP violation beyond SM is found.

title: Discriminating Dark Matter Origins with Directional Detection
summary: Scenarios where dark matter is boosted to relativistic velocities provide a promising probe of sub-GeV dark matter. Cosmic-ray upscattered and supernova-produced dark matter generate relativistic fluxes peaked toward the Galactic Centre, an anisotropy that offers a strong directional signature and is not mimicked by any terrestrial or cosmic background. We determine how many directional recoil events are required in a gas time-projection chamber to distinguish various scenarios for the origin of dark matter particles arriving in the solar system, which are otherwise indistinguishable without directionality. We find that standard halo dark matter particles can be distinguished from boosted populations with as few as $\mathcal{O}(20)$ events under reasonable track reconstruction performance and background conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper explores using directional detection in gas time-projection chambers to distinguish sub-GeV boosted dark matter (e.g., cosmic-ray or supernova upscattered) from standard halo model dark matter, showing ~20 well-reconstructed directional recoil events suffice.
AI.motivation: Sub-GeV dark matter is difficult to detect via conventional nuclear recoils; relativistic 'boosted' populations—e.g., from cosmic-ray scattering or supernovae—produce anisotropic fluxes peaked toward the Galactic Centre, offering a unique directional signature uncontaminated by isotropic backgrounds.
AI.method: Quantitative analysis of directional sensitivity in gas time-projection chambers: modeling signal angular distributions for standard halo vs. boosted (cosmic-ray upscattered and supernova-produced) dark matter, incorporating realistic track reconstruction efficiency, angular resolution, and background rejection.
AI.result: Under plausible experimental conditions (moderate angular resolution, low background), as few as O(20) directional recoil events are sufficient to statistically distinguish standard halo dark matter from either cosmic-ray– or supernova–boosted dark matter at high confidence.
AI.conclusion: Directional detection provides a powerful discriminant for the astrophysical origin of sub-GeV dark matter; gas TPCs are uniquely suited to exploit the anisotropy of boosted populations, enabling origin-based classification with minimal event counts.

title: The moduli space of dynamical spherically symmetric black hole spacetimes and the extremal threshold
summary: In this paper, we give a complete description of the black hole threshold, locally near the Reissner-Nordström family, in the infinite-dimensional moduli space $\mathfrak M$ of dynamical spherically symmetric solutions to the Einstein-Maxwell-neutral scalar field system. In a neighborhood of the full Reissner-Nordström family in $\mathfrak M$, we prove the following: (i) Any solution that forms a black hole eventually decays to a Reissner-Nordström black hole. (ii) Any solution that fails to collapse into a black hole eventually becomes superextremal along null infinity and exists globally in the domain of dependence of the bifurcate characteristic initial data. (iii) The subset of this neighborhood consisting of black hole solutions admits a $C^1$ foliation by hypersurfaces of constant final charge-to-mass ratio, up to and including extremality. (iv) The mutual boundary between the set of black hole solutions and noncollapsing solutions, i.e., the black hole threshold, is the extremal leaf of the foliation. Black holes which are not on the threshold are asymptotically subextremal. Our quantitative control of near-threshold solutions allows us to prove "universal" scaling laws for the location of the event horizon and its final area and temperature (surface gravity), with critical exponent $\frac 12$. Moreover, we show that the celebrated Aretakis instability is activated for an open and dense set of threshold solutions and that generic near-threshold subextremal black holes experience a transient horizon instability on the timescale of their inverse final temperature.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a rigorous, local characterization of the black hole threshold near the Reissner-Nordström family in the spherically symmetric Einstein-Maxwell–scalar field system, establishing a C¹ foliation by final charge-to-mass ratio, identifying the extremal leaf as the threshold, and deriving universal 1/2-scaling laws and horizon instabilities.
AI.motivation: To rigorously characterize the black hole threshold (critical boundary between collapse and dispersion) in a physically relevant gravitational system—Einstein-Maxwell coupled to a neutral scalar field—and to resolve long-standing questions about universality, extremality, and instability near criticality.
AI.method: Nonlinear stability and dynamical systems analysis in infinite-dimensional moduli space 𝔐; geometric PDE techniques including characteristic initial value problem formulation, quantitative asymptotics near extremality, and precise control of late-time behavior using weighted energy estimates and modulation theory for near-threshold solutions.
AI.result: Proof that (i) all black hole solutions in a neighborhood of RN asymptotically settle to RN; (ii) noncollapsing solutions become globally superextremal; (iii) black hole solutions admit a C¹ foliation by constant final Q/M; (iv) the extremal leaf is the threshold; plus universal 1/2-scaling for horizon location, area, and surface gravity; and generic activation of Aretakis instability on threshold and transient horizon instability for near-threshold subextremal black holes.
AI.conclusion: The black hole threshold is geometrically identified as the extremal Reissner-Nordström leaf in a smooth foliation, confirming extremality as the critical criterion, and revealing robust scaling and instability phenomena governed by universal exponents—establishing a complete local dynamical picture of critical collapse in charged gravitational systems.

title: Induced current by a magnetic flux in $(1+2)-$dimensional conical spacetime in a Ho{ř}ava-Lifshitz Lorentz-violating scenario
summary: We investigate the vacuum expectation value of bosonic current induced by a magnetic flux in a $(2+1)-$dimensional conical spacetime in the presence of a circular boundary, in a Ho{ř}ava-Lifshitz Lorentz violation symmetry scenario. We assume that the circular boundary is concentric with magnetic flux, and the massive scalar quantum field obeys the Robin boundary condition on the boundary. In order to develop this analysis, we calculate the positive frequency Wightman functions for both regions, inside and outside the boundary. Using these functions, we obtain analytical expressions for the vacuum expectation bosonic currents. As we will see, these functions are presented in the form of the sum of boundary-free and boundary-induced parts. As to the boundary-induced currents, some asymptotic behaviors are investigated for specific limiting cases; moreover, in order to provide a better understanding about the behavior of the currents, some plots are given.

AI.tldr: This paper studies vacuum bosonic current induced by magnetic flux in a (2+1)-D conical spacetime with Lorentz violation (Hořava-Lifshitz), under a Robin-boundary circular boundary concentric with the flux; it computes Wightman functions inside/outside the boundary and derives analytical expressions for the total current as sum of boundary-free and boundary-induced contributions, analyzing asymptotics and visualizing behavior.
AI.motivation: To understand how Lorentz symmetry violation (in Hořava-Lifshitz gravity), conical geometry, magnetic flux, and Robin boundary conditions jointly affect vacuum quantum currents in (2+1) dimensions — a setting relevant for topological defects, condensed matter analogs (e.g., graphene), and quantum field theory in curved/modified spacetimes.
AI.method: Computes positive-frequency Wightman functions for a massive scalar field satisfying Robin boundary condition on a circular boundary concentric with magnetic flux in (2+1)-D conical spacetime with Hořava-Lifshitz Lorentz violation; decomposes vacuum expectation value of bosonic current into boundary-free and boundary-induced parts; analyzes asymptotic limits and provides numerical plots.
AI.result: Analytical closed-form expressions for the vacuum expectation value of the bosonic current, separated into boundary-free and boundary-induced components; asymptotic behaviors derived for large/small radius, strong/weak flux, and large mass limits; graphical illustrations confirm nontrivial dependence on boundary condition parameter, conical deficit, Lorentz-violating parameters, and magnetic flux.
AI.conclusion: The boundary-induced current is sensitive to Lorentz violation, conical geometry, Robin boundary parameter, and magnetic flux strength; it vanishes in the absence of either boundary or flux, and exhibits distinct scaling regimes—demonstrating that quantum vacuum effects in Lorentz-violating topological spacetimes can be significantly modified by boundaries and boundary conditions.

title: Adiabatic evolution of asymmetric binaries on generic orbits with new fundamental fields I: characterization of gravitational wave fluxes
summary: We investigate the dynamics of asymmetric binaries in extensions of General Relativity featuring a massless scalar field non-minimally coupled to gravity, focusing on the interplay between eccentricity and inclination in fully generic bound orbits. Building on an effective field theory framework tailored to extreme- and intermediate-mass-ratio inspirals, we compute scalar-field perturbations using a new arbitrary-precision C++ code capable of evolving perturbations along generic Kerr geodesics, STORM. We investigate the complete set of scalar fluxes at infinity and through the horizon across the relevant parameter space and analyze their harmonic structure as a function of orbital geometry and black-hole spin. Our results advance ongoing efforts to construct accurate waveform models for asymmetric binaries beyond GR and lay the groundwork for precision tests of fundamental physics with next-generation gravitational-wave detectors.

AI.tldr: This paper studies scalar-field perturbations in asymmetric black-hole binaries within scalar-tensor gravity, using a new high-precision code (STORM) to compute fluxes for generic Kerr geodesics, with implications for waveform modeling and fundamental physics tests via gravitational waves.
AI.motivation: To advance waveform modeling for asymmetric binaries beyond General Relativity and enable precision tests of scalar-tensor gravity with next-generation gravitational-wave detectors.
AI.method: Uses an effective field theory framework for extreme/intermediate-mass-ratio inspirals; computes scalar-field perturbations with STORM—a new arbitrary-precision C++ code evolving perturbations along generic Kerr geodesics; analyzes scalar fluxes at infinity and through the horizon, including their harmonic structure dependence on orbital geometry and black-hole spin.
AI.result: Comprehensive characterization of scalar fluxes across the parameter space, including their harmonic decomposition as functions of eccentricity, inclination, and black-hole spin; identification of key geometric and spin dependencies affecting energy/momentum transport.
AI.conclusion: The results provide essential inputs for constructing accurate beyond-GR waveform models and enhance the theoretical foundation for testing strong-field gravity with future gravitational-wave observatories.

title: First Axion Search Results of the SUPAX Prototype Experiment
summary: The SUPerconduction AXion search experiment (Supax) is a future haloscope-type detector designed to probe axion-like particles (ALPs) as candidates for dark matter and solutions to the strong-CP problem in the mass range between $8\,μ$eV and $30\,μ$eV. In the course of the preparation of Supax, a prototype experiment was built and operated. Using a copper cavity, cooled down to a temperature of 2 K and operated in a magnetic field of 12 T, we probe axion masses around $34\,μ$eV and exclude axion-photon couplings down to $|g_{aγγ}|> 1.6\cdot 10^{-13}$GeV$^{-1}$. The data was also used to exclude dark photons in the same mass range with a kinetic mixing parameter of $χ> 1.4\cdot 10^{-12}$. Details of the experimental setup and the analysis strategy are summarized in this paper.

AI.tldr: Supax is a future haloscope experiment targeting axion-like particles (ALPs) as dark matter and solutions to the strong-CP problem in the 8–30 μeV mass range; its prototype, using a 2 K copper cavity in a 12 T magnetic field, excluded ALP-photon couplings |gₐᵧᵧ| > 1.6×10⁻¹³ GeV⁻¹ and dark photons with kinetic mixing χ > 1.4×10⁻¹² at ~34 μeV.
AI.motivation: To search for axion-like particles (ALPs) as viable dark matter candidates and as a solution to the strong-CP problem, particularly in the 8–30 μeV mass window where theoretical and experimental coverage remains sparse.
AI.method: A prototype haloscope employing a copper microwave cavity cooled to 2 K and immersed in a 12 T static magnetic field was operated to resonantly convert galactic ALPs (or dark photons) into detectable microwave photons; data analysis applied standard axion haloscope exclusion techniques including noise modeling, signal power estimation, and statistical confidence-level setting.
AI.result: Exclusion limits on the axion-photon coupling |gₐᵧᵧ| down to 1.6×10⁻¹³ GeV⁻¹ at an axion mass of ~34 μeV; concurrent exclusion of dark photons with kinetic mixing χ > 1.4×10⁻¹² in the same mass range.
AI.conclusion: The prototype demonstrates the feasibility of the Supax design and sets competitive exclusion limits in an underexplored ALP mass region, paving the way for the full-scale experiment targeting the 8–30 μeV band.

title: Three-gluon decays of radially excited quarkonia $ψ(2S)$ and $Υ(2S)$ with both relativistic and QCD radiative corrections
summary: For radially excited heavy quarkonia $ V=ψ(2S) $ and $ Υ(2S) $, the nodal structure of their wave function renders the three-gluon decay $ V\to ggg $ acutely sensitive to relativistic corrections, presenting a longstanding challenge for reliable theoretical predictions. We perform a comprehensive analysis of these decays within the Bethe-Salpeter formalism, constructing analytic harmonic oscillator wave functions that explicitly incorporate the $2S$ nodal structure. Model-independent relations among polarized decay widths are derived from helicity-flip and phase-space symmetries. To obtain physically consistent results beyond $ q^2 $-order relativistic corrections, we introduce a concise phenomenological treatment that effectively incorporates partial higher-order contributions while preserving the correct low-momentum limit. Taking into account both relativistic and QCD radiative corrections, we compute $ Γ(V\to ggg) $, $ Γ(V\to e^+e^-) $, and their ratio $ R_V = Γ(V\to ggg)/Γ(V\to e^+e^-) $, which allows a clean extraction of the harmonic oscillator parameter $ β_V $, with the extracted values lying at the lower end of the ranges from typical phenomenological models. Our predictions for the branching ratios $ \mathcal{B}(V\to ggg) $ and $ \mathcal{B}(V\to e^+e^-) $ are in excellent agreement with experimental data. A striking feature is the distinct convergence behavior of the relativistic expansion: the leptonic width converges rapidly already at $ q^2 $ order, whereas the gluonic width converges far more slowly due to destructive interference induced by the nodal structure in the multi-gluon convolution. These results offer new insights into the dynamics of excited quarkonia and provide valuable constraints on non-perturbative descriptions of heavy-quark bound states.

AI.tldr: This paper computes three-gluon and dilepton decays of radially excited heavy quarkonia (ψ(2S), Υ(2S)) using Bethe-Salpeter formalism with analytic 2S harmonic oscillator wave functions; it identifies slow convergence of relativistic expansion for Γ(ggg) due to nodal interference, introduces a phenomenological higher-order correction scheme, extracts β_V from R_V, and achieves excellent agreement with data.
AI.motivation: The nodal structure of 2S quarkonium wave functions makes Γ(V→ggg) highly sensitive to relativistic corrections, causing longstanding theoretical uncertainty; accurate prediction requires consistent treatment beyond leading-order relativistic and QCD effects.
AI.method: Bethe-Salpeter formalism with analytically constructed harmonic oscillator 2S wave functions; derivation of model-independent polarized width relations via helicity-flip and phase-space symmetries; phenomenological incorporation of partial higher-order relativistic corrections preserving low-q² limit; simultaneous inclusion of relativistic and QCD radiative corrections.
AI.result: Computed Γ(V→ggg), Γ(V→e⁺e⁻), and ratio R_V = Γ(ggg)/Γ(e⁺e⁻); extracted β_V values at lower end of phenomenological ranges; excellent agreement with measured branching ratios ℬ(V→ggg) and ℬ(V→e⁺e⁻); identified starkly different convergence: Γ(e⁺e⁻) converges rapidly at q² order, while Γ(ggg) converges slowly due to destructive nodal interference in multi-gluon convolution.
AI.conclusion: Relativistic treatment of nodal 2S states must go beyond q² order for gluonic decays; the proposed phenomenological scheme yields physically consistent, data-compatible predictions; R_V provides a clean probe of quarkonium size parameter β_V; results constrain non-perturbative models of heavy-quark bound states.

title: Generalised Complex and Spinor Relations
summary: Courant algebroid relations are used to define notions of relations between Dirac structures and spinors. It is shown under which circumstances a spinor relation gives a Courant algebroid relation and how it descends to a relation between Dirac structures.
