A converse to this result is proved: a T-duality relation induces a spinor relation that links the Dirac generating operators defining T-dual Courant algebroids, generalising the isomorphism of twisted cohomology associated with topological T-duality. We introduce the notion of relation between generalised complex structures and characterise their reduction. We also define relations between generalised Kähler structures, and rephrase them in terms of bi-Hermitian structures which induce T-duality relations between $\mathcal{N}=(2,2)$ supersymmetric sigma-models. We prove existence results for T-dual structures, and demonstrate compatibility of T-duality relations with Type II supergravity equations.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a geometric framework for T-duality using Courant algebroid and spinor relations, establishes equivalences between Dirac, generalized complex, and generalized Kähler structures under duality, and proves compatibility with Type II supergravity.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified, geometric characterization of T-duality beyond topological settings—extending twisted cohomology isomorphisms to spinor-level correspondences and incorporating dynamical (supergravity) constraints.
AI.method: Uses Courant algebroid relations and spinor correspondences to define relations between Dirac structures, generalized complex structures, and generalized Kähler structures; employs reduction theory and bi-Hermitian geometry to relate dual sigma-models; verifies compatibility with supergravity via constraint analysis on the generalized metric and fluxes.
AI.result: 1) Spinor relations induce Courant algebroid relations and descend to Dirac structure relations; 2) T-duality induces a canonical spinor relation linking Dirac generating operators; 3) Generalized complex/Kähler relations are characterized and reduced; 4) Bi-Hermitian structures encoding N=(2,2) sigma-models are shown to be T-dual; 5) Existence of T-dual generalized geometries is proved; 6) T-duality relations preserve Type II supergravity equations.
AI.conclusion: T-duality is fully captured at the level of generalized geometry via Courant–spinor correspondence, enabling consistent lifting from topological to differential-geometric and physical (supergravity) contexts.

title: Regge's Inferno
summary: We study large-spin operators in conformal field theories (CFTs) in spacetime dimensions $d>2$ by placing the theory on appropriate pp-wave backgrounds. We show that these geometries admit Heisenberg-group symmetries, and that these symmetries, combined with locality of quantum fields on such spacetimes, impose strong constraints on the asymptotic spectrum in the large-spin limit. The pp-wave backgrounds probe both the small-twist regime, corresponding to the Regge or light-cone bootstrap, and a strongly coupled regime of large twist. Finally, we demonstrate that causality (or the requirement that the energy be bounded from below) leads to a new unitarity bound in $3+1$ dimensions.

AI.tldr: This paper studies large-spin operators in CFTs in d > 2 using pp-wave backgrounds, revealing Heisenberg-group symmetries that constrain the asymptotic spectrum; it connects to both Regge/light-cone and strong-coupling regimes and derives a new unitarity bound from causality in 3+1D.
AI.motivation: To understand the large-spin asymptotics of CFT spectra beyond standard light-cone or Regge limits, leveraging geometric backgrounds that encode both small- and large-twist physics while respecting fundamental principles like locality and causality.
AI.method: Analyzing CFTs placed on pp-wave spacetime backgrounds; identifying emergent Heisenberg-group isometries; combining symmetry constraints with quantum field locality and causality (energy boundedness) to derive spectral constraints and bounds.
AI.result: Strong asymptotic constraints on large-spin operator spectra; unified description of small-twist (Regge/light-cone) and large-twist regimes; derivation of a novel unitarity bound in 3+1 dimensions enforced by causality.
AI.conclusion: Pp-wave geometries provide a powerful geometric tool for probing universal features of CFTs at large spin; symmetry and causality jointly fix nontrivial constraints on operator dimensions, extending known bootstrap bounds into new kinematic regimes.

title: Quantum Dynamics of the Schwarzschild Interior in Ashtekar-Barbero Variables with Minimal Length Effects
summary:
We study the quantum dynamics of the Schwarzschild interior in the Ashtekar-Barbero formulation, focusing on the fate of the classical singularity and the annihilation-to-nothing scenario. Using minisuperspace Wheeler-DeWitt quantization, we first analyze the standard Schrödinger representation and show that the annihilation-to-nothing behavior appears only for a specific choice of factor ordering and is not generic.
  We then introduce a generalized uncertainty principle (GUP), which induces minimal-length effects through a deformation of the canonical algebra. Solving the modified Wheeler-DeWitt equation and constructing Gaussian wave packets localized at the horizon, we find that the annihilation-to-nothing behavior is suppressed once the GUP corrections are included. Our results indicate that minimal-length effects qualitatively alter the quantum interior dynamics and challenge the robustness of this scenario as a mechanism for singularity resolution.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates quantum dynamics inside a Schwarzschild black hole using Ashtekar-Barbero variables and minisuperspace Wheeler-DeWitt quantization. It shows that the 'annihilation-to-nothing' scenario is non-generic in standard quantization (dependent on factor ordering), and is suppressed when a generalized uncertainty principle (GUP) introducing minimal-length effects is included—suggesting GUP qualitatively alters interior dynamics and undermines annihilation-to-nothing as a robust singularity resolution mechanism.
AI.motivation: To assess the robustness of the 'annihilation-to-nothing' scenario as a quantum resolution of the Schwarzschild interior singularity, particularly under canonical quantization ambiguities and Planck-scale corrections.
AI.method: Minisuperspace Wheeler-DeWitt quantization in the Ashtekar-Barbero framework; analysis of factor-ordering dependence in the Schrödinger representation; incorporation of a generalized uncertainty principle (GUP) deforming the canonical commutation relation; numerical/analytical solution of the modified Wheeler-DeWitt equation; construction and evolution of Gaussian wave packets localized at the horizon.
AI.result: The annihilation-to-nothing behavior is shown to be non-generic—occurring only for a specific factor ordering in standard quantization; inclusion of GUP-induced minimal-length effects suppresses this behavior entirely; wave packet evolution reveals qualitatively altered quantum interior dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Minimal-length effects from GUP fundamentally modify quantum gravitational dynamics inside Schwarzschild black holes, challenging the viability and robustness of annihilation-to-nothing as a singularity resolution mechanism.

title: Search for Z' bosons decaying into charginos in final states with two oppositely charged leptons and missing transverse momentum in pp collisions at $\sqrt{s}$ = 13 TeV
comment: Submitted to the Journal of High Energy Physics. All figures and tables can be found at http://cms-results.web.cern.ch/cms-results/public-results/publications/SUS-23-006 (CMS Public Pages)
summary: Massive leptophobic Z' bosons decaying to a pair of charginos are searched for in proton-proton collisions at $\sqrt{s}$ = 13 TeV, using data samples collected by the CMS experiment in 2016, 2017, and 2018, corresponding to a total integrated luminosity of 138 fb$^{-1}$. The Z' bosons originate from an additional U(1)' gauge symmetry extended to the minimal supersymmetric standard model. The final state consists of two oppositely charged leptons and missing transverse momentum. The signal extraction is performed with a parametrized neural network. The measurements are found to be consistent with the standard model expectations. Upper limits are set on the Z' boson production cross sections as a function of the Z' and chargino masses. The analysis excludes Z' boson masses up to about 3.5 TeV for the specific case of Z' bosons decaying exclusively to charginos, with the charginos decaying to W bosons and neutralinos.

AI.tldr: Search for leptophobic Z' bosons decaying to chargino pairs in CMS data (138 fb⁻¹ at 13 TeV); no excess observed; Z' masses up to ~3.5 TeV excluded for exclusive chargino decay to W + neutralino.
AI.motivation: Test extensions of the minimal supersymmetric standard model (MSSM) featuring an additional U(1)' gauge symmetry predicting massive, leptophobic Z' bosons that couple preferentially to charginos — a probe of supersymmetry and hidden-sector gauge dynamics.
AI.method: Analyzed pp collision data at √s = 13 TeV (CMS, 2016–2018, 138 fb⁻¹); selected events with two opposite-sign leptons and large missing transverse momentum; employed a parametrized neural network for signal-background discrimination; performed statistical inference to set limits on Z' production cross section.
AI.result: No significant deviation from Standard Model background expectations; upper limits placed on σ(pp → Z') × BR(Z' → χ⁺χ⁻) as a function of m_Z′ and m_χ±; for Z' → χ⁺χ⁻ → (W⁺χ⁰)(W⁻χ⁰), Z' masses below ~3.5 TeV are excluded at 95% CL.
AI.conclusion: No evidence found for the hypothesized leptophobic Z' boson in this decay channel; results constrain parameter space of U(1)'-extended MSSM, particularly models where Z' couples dominantly to charginos and decays exclusively to them.

title: Heavy-quark contributions to the polarized DIS structure functions at NLO in the ACOT scheme
summary: This study explores the heavy-quark contributions to polarized structure functions in deep-inelastic scattering at next-to-leading order. The structure functions $g_1$, $g_4$, $g_5$, $g_6$, and $g_7$ are computed, while $g_2$ and $g_3$ are excluded due to the higher-twist suppression. The calculations are performed within the ACOT renormalization scheme, which ensures theoretical consistency across kinematic regions where heavy quarks transition from being dynamically produced to fully active degrees of freedom. By incorporating heavy-flavor contributions at next-to-leading-order, this work provides deeper insights into their role in polarized structure functions and the spin-dependent dynamics of QCD. Both analytical results and their numerical implementation are presented.

AI.tldr: This paper computes next-to-leading-order heavy-quark contributions to polarized structure functions $g_1$, $g_4$–$g_7$ in deep-inelastic scattering using the ACOT renormalization scheme, excluding $g_2$, $g_3$ due to higher-twist suppression; it delivers analytical and numerical results advancing QCD spin dynamics understanding.
AI.motivation: To systematically incorporate heavy-quark effects—particularly their transition from dynamically produced to active degrees of freedom—into polarized structure functions at next-to-leading order, thereby improving theoretical consistency and physical interpretation in spin-dependent QCD.
AI.method: Analytical computation of polarized structure functions $g_1$, $g_4$, $g_5$, $g_6$, $g_7$ at next-to-leading order in QCD within the ACOT (Aivazis-Collins-Olness-Tung) renormalization scheme; higher-twist-suppressed functions $g_2$, $g_3$ are omitted.
AI.result: Complete NLO heavy-quark contributions to the specified polarized structure functions; both closed-form analytical expressions and numerically implementable results are obtained, ensuring smooth matching across heavy-quark mass thresholds.
AI.conclusion: Heavy-quark contributions significantly affect polarized structure functions beyond leading order; the ACOT scheme provides a consistent framework for describing their kinematic evolution, enhancing predictive power for spin-dependent DIS observables.

title: Non-Lorentzian Supergravity from Matrix Theory
summary: It was recently shown that the decoupling limits leading to matrix (gauge) theories on D-branes give rise to non-Lorentzian target space geometries. Perturbatively, matrix theory describes a quantum gravity theory whose low-energy supergravity description exhibits non-Lorentzian behavior. Focusing on the D-particle case associated with the Banks-Fischler-Shenker-Susskind matrix theory, and using techniques from ambitwistor string theory, we show evidence that the dynamics of this non-Lorentzian gravity should be related to anomalies in the current algebra of the associated fundamental string worldsheet theory. At large N, the D-particle backreaction deforms the non-Lorentzian supergravity to the Lorentzian IIA theory, providing a holographic description of the BFSS matrix theory. At a moderately large N such that the D-particles decouple at the leading order, this non-Lorentzian supergravity maps holographically to the leading-order contribution of weakly coupled bulk gravity. This approximately non-Lorentzian regime is related to the null reduction of eleven-dimensional supergravity. Within the non-Lorentzian supergravity, non-trivial dynamics arises from the backreaction of extended brane objects that form BPS states with the D-particles. Finally, we generalize these results to other D-brane and string soliton holographic constructions.

AI.tldr: This paper links non-Lorentzian supergravity emerging from BFSS matrix theory (D-particle limit) to worldsheet current algebra anomalies via ambitwistor string techniques; shows holographic emergence of Lorentzian IIA at large N, and identifies the null-reduced 11D supergravity as the approximate low-energy description in the decoupled D-particle regime.
AI.motivation: To understand the geometric and dynamical origin of non-Lorentzian target space geometries arising in the decoupling limits of D-brane matrix theories—particularly BFSS—and to establish a holographic and anomaly-based characterization of their quantum gravity content.
AI.method: Applies ambitwistor string theory techniques to the D-particle (BFSS) matrix theory; analyzes current algebra anomalies on the fundamental string worldsheet; studies D-particle backreaction and its large-N and moderate-N holographic limits; performs null reduction of eleven-dimensional supergravity to match the non-Lorentzian supergravity regime; generalizes findings to other D-brane and string soliton setups.
AI.result: Evidence that non-Lorentzian gravity dynamics in BFSS is governed by worldsheet current algebra anomalies; identification of the large-N limit as recovering Lorentzian IIA supergravity holographically; demonstration that moderate-N decoupled regime maps to weakly coupled bulk gravity described by null-reduced 11D supergravity; characterization of BPS extended brane backreaction as source of non-trivial dynamics within the non-Lorentzian framework.
AI.conclusion: The non-Lorentzian structure in BFSS matrix theory has a precise worldsheet anomaly origin and admits a consistent holographic interpretation: it flows to Lorentzian IIA at large N, while retaining a well-defined, approximately non-Lorentzian gravitational description—null-reduced 11D supergravity—at finite but moderately large N, with extended BPS branes driving its dynamics.

title: Fragmentation contributions to transverse nucleon spin observables in semi-inclusive deep-inelastic scattering at NLO
summary: We study the spin-dependent cross section in semi-inclusive deep-inelastic lepton-nucleon collisions, $\ell + N^\uparrow\to \ell+h+X$. We focus on the cross section that is integrated over the transverse momentum $P_{h\perp}$ of the detected unpolarized hadron. We analyze this cross section at large virtuality $Q^2$ of the exchanged virtual photon within the framework of collinear twist-3 factorization in perturbative QCD. The two main transverse spin observables, the single nucleon spin asymmetry (SSA) and the double longitudinal lepton-transverse nucleon spin asymmetry (DSA), both receive contributions from chiral-odd twist-3 three-parton fragmentation functions. We study these fragmentation contributions to Next-To-Leading Order (NLO) in perturbative QCD. We explicitly observe that collinear twist-3 factorization holds for these contributions at the one-loop level. We confront our NLO formulae with HERMES data and provide numerical predictions for EIC kinematics.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes spin-dependent cross sections in semi-inclusive deep-inelastic scattering (SIDIS) with transversely polarized nucleons, focusing on collinear twist-3 factorization contributions from chiral-odd three-parton fragmentation functions to SSA and DSA at NLO QCD; it verifies factorization at one-loop level and compares results with HERMES data while predicting EIC observables.
AI.motivation: To systematically compute and validate the role of chiral-odd twist-3 three-parton fragmentation functions in transverse-spin observables (SSA and DSA) within collinear twist-3 factorization, especially beyond leading order.
AI.method: Collinear twist-3 factorization in perturbative QCD; analytical one-loop calculation of chiral-odd three-parton fragmentation function contributions to the $P_{h\perp}$-integrated cross section; comparison with HERMES experimental data; numerical predictions for Electron-Ion Collider (EIC) kinematics.
AI.result: Demonstration that collinear twist-3 factorization holds for chiral-odd fragmentation contributions at NLO (one-loop); derivation of complete NLO formulae for SSA and DSA; quantitative agreement with HERMES data; first NLO predictions for EIC spin asymmetries.
AI.conclusion: Chiral-odd twist-3 three-parton fragmentation functions provide a consistent and quantitatively relevant mechanism for transverse-spin asymmetries in SIDIS; their NLO treatment is theoretically sound and phenomenologically viable for current and future experiments.

title: Efficient Conformal Block Evaluation with GoBlocks
summary: Conformal blocks in odd spacetime dimensions are not known in closed analytic form. To facilitate efficient computations in the conformal bootstrap, we introduce $\texttt{GoBlocks}$: a novel conformal-block generator implemented in the Go programming language, designed for rapid, on-the-fly, parallel evaluation using recursive relations. The package supports both multi-point and derivative-based bootstrap approaches and allows flexible control over accuracy and performance. We benchmark $\texttt{GoBlocks}$ against the $\texttt{scalar_blocks}$ package, finding significant speed improvements in applications where computational speed and moderate accuracy are critical, but ultra-high precision is not essential. As an illustration, we apply $\texttt{GoBlocks}$ to the mixed-correlator bootstrap of the three-dimensional Ising model, formulated as a non-convex optimisation problem in a suitable truncation scheme. We simultaneously optimise over external scaling dimensions and OPE CFT data. In addition, we discuss how the approach scales as we increase the number of mixed correlators in more general $O(N)$ vector models.

AI.tldr: Introduces GoBlocks, a fast, parallel Go-based conformal block evaluator for odd-dimensional CFT bootstrap, benchmarked against scalar_blocks and applied to 3D Ising and O(N) models.
AI.motivation: Conformal blocks in odd spacetime dimensions lack closed-form analytic expressions, hindering efficient conformal bootstrap computations; existing tools are insufficiently fast or flexible for large-scale or on-the-fly evaluations.
AI.method: Develops GoBlocks—a Go-language package implementing recursive relations for rapid, parallel, on-the-fly evaluation of conformal blocks in odd dimensions; supports multi-point and derivative bootstrap, adjustable accuracy–speed trade-offs, and joint optimization over scaling dimensions and OPE data in mixed-correlator setups.
AI.result: GoBlocks achieves significant speedups over scalar_blocks in moderate-accuracy regimes; successfully applied to non-convex optimization of the 3D Ising model’s mixed-correlator bootstrap and scalability analysis extended to general O(N) vector models.
AI.conclusion: GoBlocks enables scalable, high-throughput conformal bootstrap studies in odd dimensions, particularly where speed and flexibility outweigh the need for ultra-high numerical precision.

title: Polymerized spacetime dynamics with multi-field source: unraveling the pre-inflationary Universe
summary: We study a multi-field model in Loop Quantum Cosmology for a maximally symmetric spacetime governed by the Einstein--Hilbert action minimally coupled to scalar fields. Using a Legendre transformation, we formulate the Hamiltonian dynamics in canonically equivalent geometrodynamical and Yang--Mills--type representations, incorporating nontrivial couplings through a geometric structure on the multi-field configuration space. Implementing the $\barμ$-scheme polymerization, we obtain the loop-quantum-corrected Friedmann equations. By focusing on the two-field model as an example, we analyze the effective dynamics for specific potentials. The \textit{quantum bouncing, transition, and slow-roll inflationary} phases are investigated numerically, and viability of the models is assessed by evaluating the number of e-folds during the inflationary phase for certain given initial conditions. The global behavior of the background evolution is further examined through linear stability and dynamical-systems analyses.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a multi-field loop quantum cosmology model for maximally symmetric spacetimes, formulates Hamiltonian dynamics in both geometrodynamical and Yang–Mills–type representations via Legendre transformation, implements the $\bar{\mu}$-scheme polymerization to derive quantum-corrected Friedmann equations, and numerically investigates quantum bouncing, transition, and slow-roll inflation phases—particularly in a two-field setup—with viability assessed via e-fold counts and global behavior analyzed using linear stability and dynamical systems methods.
AI.motivation: To extend Loop Quantum Cosmology (LQC) to multi-scalar-field models with nontrivial field-space geometry, enabling consistent incorporation of quantum gravity corrections into early-universe inflationary dynamics while preserving canonical structure and addressing limitations of single-field LQC.
AI.method: Legendre transformation to construct canonically equivalent geometrodynamical and Yang–Mills–type Hamiltonian formulations; $\bar{\mu}$-scheme polymerization for loop quantization; numerical integration of effective equations for two-field potentials; linear stability analysis and dynamical-systems techniques (e.g., fixed-point classification, phase-space trajectories) to study global background evolution.
AI.result: Derivation of loop-quantum-corrected Friedmann equations for generic multi-field models; identification and characterization of three distinct cosmological phases—quantum bounce, transition, and slow-roll inflation—in two-field examples; quantitative e-fold estimates satisfying observational constraints for suitable initial conditions and potentials; classification of fixed points and stability properties revealing robustness of inflationary attractors and avoidance of singularities.
AI.conclusion: Multi-field LQC with geometric field-space couplings admits well-behaved, singularity-free background evolution featuring observationally viable inflation; the Yang–Mills–type representation offers a novel perspective facilitating quantization and reveals structural parallels between gravitational and gauge-theoretic dynamics in quantum cosmology.

title: Analysis of the hidden-charm pentaquark candidates in the $J/ψΣ$ mass spectrum via the QCD sum rules
summary: In this work, we adopt the diquark model, construct the diquark-diquark-antiquark type currents with the light quarks $uus$ in two octets, and study the $uusc\bar{c}$ pentaquark states in the framework of the QCD sum rules systematically. We obtain the mass spectrum of the hidden-charm-singly-strange pentaquark states with the quantum numbers $IJ^{P}=1{\frac{1}{2}}^-$, $1{\frac{3}{2}}^-$ and $1{\frac{5}{2}}^-$. And we can search for those $P_{cs}$ states in the processes $Σ_b^+\to P_{cs}^+φ\to J/ψΣ^+ \,φ$ and $Ξ_b^0\to P_{cs}^+ K^- \to J/ψΣ^+\, K^-$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies hidden-charm-singly-strange pentaquark states (uusc̄c) using diquark-diquark-antiquark currents and QCD sum rules, predicting masses for J^P = 1/2^−, 3/2^−, 5/2^− states and suggesting experimental search channels.
AI.motivation: To systematically investigate the mass spectrum and quantum numbers of the recently observed or predicted hidden-charm-singly-strange pentaquark states (P_{cs}) within the diquark model and QCD sum rules framework.
AI.method: Constructs diquark-diquark-antiquark-type interpolating currents with uus light-quark content in two SU(3) octets; applies QCD sum rules to compute the mass spectrum of uusc̄c pentaquarks with IJ^P = 1(1/2)^−, 1(3/2)^−, 1(5/2)^−.
AI.result: Predicts the mass spectrum for the uusc̄c pentaquark states with negative parity and total isospin I = 1; identifies viable production and decay channels: Σ_b^+ → P_{cs}^+ φ → J/ψ Σ^+ φ and Ξ_b^0 → P_{cs}^+ K^− → J/ψ Σ^+ K^−.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical mass predictions support the existence of several P_{cs} states with distinct spin-parity assignments, and the proposed decay channels provide concrete strategies for experimental detection at LHCb or future facilities.

title: Thermodynamically massless Simpson-Visser black holes
summary: In this work, we scrutinize the thermodynamic properties of the Simpson-Visser (SV) spacetime. Working within Einstein gravity coupled to nonlinear electrodynamics (NLED) and a scalar field with negative kinetic energy, we rederive the solution in a formulation where the integration constants do not explicitly appear in the action, allowing them to vary consistently in the thermodynamic analysis. Using the Euclidean method, we show that the regular spacetime structure modifies the boundary contributions to the conserved charge associated with time translations, allowing the NLED sector to cancel the mass term and yielding a black hole with vanishing thermodynamic mass. Nevertheless, the spacetime admits a conserved magnetic charge and describes a regular black hole with a single horizon, finite temperature, and entropy, while the first law of thermodynamics holds in a modified form. We further compare this solution with the corresponding scalar-free singular black hole obtained when the regular parameter vanishes. Placing the two configurations in the same heat bath with identical temperature and magnetic chemical potential, we find that the SV regular black hole always has a larger free energy, indicating that the scalar-free singular configuration is thermodynamically preferred.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes thermodynamic properties of the Simpson-Visser spacetime in Einstein gravity coupled to nonlinear electrodynamics and a ghost scalar field; it finds a regular black hole with vanishing thermodynamic mass but finite temperature, entropy, and magnetic charge, satisfying a modified first law; comparison shows the singular (scalar-free) counterpart is thermodynamically favored in shared thermal equilibrium.
AI.motivation: To consistently perform thermodynamic analysis of regular black holes by ensuring integration constants vary freely—requiring reformulation of the action—and to assess thermodynamic stability and preference between regular and singular black hole configurations.
AI.method: Reformulates the Simpson-Visser solution within Einstein–NLED–ghost scalar theory such that integration constants are not fixed in the action; applies the Euclidean path integral method to compute boundary contributions to conserved charges, derive thermodynamic quantities (mass, temperature, entropy, magnetic charge), verify the first law, and compare free energies of regular and singular configurations in a common heat bath.
AI.result: The SV black hole has zero thermodynamic mass due to cancellation between gravitational and NLED boundary terms, yet retains finite temperature, entropy, and magnetic charge; it satisfies a modified first law; its free energy exceeds that of the singular limit under identical temperature and magnetic chemical potential.
AI.conclusion: Although the Simpson-Visser spacetime yields a physically well-behaved (regular, single-horizon) black hole, thermodynamic analysis reveals the singular scalar-free solution is preferred—implying regularization via this ghost-scalar–NLED mechanism does not yield a globally stable ground state.

title: Systematic exploration of triply heavy tetraquarks: spectroscopic and decay characteristics
summary: While hidden, singly, doubly, and fully heavy tetraquark states have been experimentally observed, triply heavy tetraquark states remain unconfirmed. We systematically investigate the spectroscopic and decay properties of four triply heavy-flavor tetraquark systems ($cc\bar{c}\bar{n}$, $cc\bar{c}\bar{s}$, $bb\bar{b}\bar{n}$, $bb\bar{b}\bar{s}$; $n=u,d$) based on the nonrelativistic quark model. Using an effective Hamiltonian, we employ the Gaussian expansion method to solve the four-body Schrödinger equation and incorporate the effect of color-spin configuration mixing. Results show both $cc\bar{c}\bar{q}$ and $bb\bar{b}\bar{q}$ systems have two $J^{P}=0^{+}$, three $J^{P}=1^{+}$, and one $J^{P}=2^{+}$ states, with ground-state masses of 5.2-5.5 GeV and 15.0-15.3 GeV, respectively. Root-mean-square radius analysis supports compact tetraquark configurations. All states are unstable, with rearrangement strong decays dominant and negligible radiative decays. Narrow resonances (e.g., $T_{c^{2}\bar{c}\bar{s}}(5360,0^{+})$, $T_{b^{2}\bar{b}\bar{n}}(15052,0^{+})$) arise from Feynman amplitude cancellation. We propose experimental searches in $J/ψD^{*}_{s}$/ $η_{c}D_{s}$ (5.3-5.4 GeV) and $ΥB^{*}$ (15.0-15.1 GeV) channels, providing key guidance for triply heavy tetraquark identification.

AI.tldr: This paper theoretically investigates triply heavy tetraquark states (e.g., $cc\bar{c}\bar{q}$, $bb\bar{b}\bar{q}$) using the nonrelativistic quark model and Gaussian expansion method; predicts compact, unstable states with specific $J^P$ quantum numbers, narrow resonances due to amplitude cancellation, and proposes key experimental channels for detection.
AI.motivation: Triply heavy tetraquark states remain experimentally unconfirmed despite observations of other heavy tetraquarks; their systematic theoretical study is needed to guide experimental searches and deepen understanding of QCD multiquark dynamics.
AI.method: Nonrelativistic quark model with effective Hamiltonian; Gaussian expansion method to solve the four-body Schrödinger equation; inclusion of color-spin configuration mixing; root-mean-square radius analysis; decay width estimation via rearrangement strong decays and radiative transitions.
AI.result: Predicted two $0^+$, three $1^+$, and one $2^+$ states for both $cc\bar{c}\bar{q}$ (masses 5.2–5.5 GeV) and $bb\bar{b}\bar{q}$ (15.0–15.3 GeV); all states are compact and unstable; dominant strong decays via rearrangement; narrow resonances identified (e.g., $T_{c^{2}\bar{c}\bar{s}}(5360,0^{+})$, $T_{b^{2}\bar{b}\bar{n}}(15052,0^{+})$) due to Feynman amplitude cancellation; proposed experimental channels: $J/\psi D^*_s/\eta_c D_s$ (5.3–5.4 GeV) and $\Upsilon B^*$ (15.0–15.1 GeV).
AI.conclusion: Triply heavy tetraquarks are viable compact hadrons with distinctive spectroscopic patterns and narrow widths arising from dynamical suppression of decays; the predicted masses and decay channels provide concrete, testable signatures for ongoing and future high-energy experiments (e.g., LHCb, Belle II, CMS).

title: Nucleating an Inflationary Universe: Euclidean Wormholes and their No-Boundary Limit
summary: No-boundary instantons and Euclidean "wineglass" wormholes have both been proposed as providing suitable initial conditions for the current expanding phase of our universe, and in particular for providing conditions that are favorable to an inflationary phase. These finite action solutions have generally been regarded as unrelated, and enacting different scenarios - in one case the creation of spacetime from nothing, and in the other up-tunneling from a Euclidean Anti-de Sitter vacuum. By studying explicit solutions of both axionic and magnetic wineglass wormholes, we find that in the zero-charge limit the throat of the wormholes pinches off, leaving a no-boundary instanton that disconnects from the asymptotic Anti-de Sitter region. Thus wormholes and no-boundary instantons are part of a common family of Euclidean solutions. Along the way, we resolve the long-known puzzle that the action of wineglass wormholes can become negative. Moreover, small-charge wormholes lead to a longer inflationary phase than large-charge solutions, while no-boundary instantons dominate the probability distribution overall.

AI.tldr: This paper unifies no-boundary instantons and Euclidean 'wineglass' wormholes as limiting cases of a common family of Euclidean solutions, showing that zero-charge wormholes reduce to no-boundary instantons; it resolves the negative-action puzzle for wineglass wormholes and finds that small-charge wormholes yield longer inflation, though no-boundary instantons dominate the probability distribution.
AI.motivation: To reconcile two seemingly distinct proposals for quantum cosmological initial conditions—no-boundary instantons (creation from nothing) and wineglass wormholes (up-tunneling from Euclidean AdS)—and resolve the long-standing puzzle of negative Euclidean action in wineglass wormholes.
AI.method: Analytical study of explicit axionic and magnetic wineglass wormhole solutions in Euclidean gravity, focusing on the zero- and small-charge limits; examination of throat geometry, action evaluation, and semiclassical probability weighting via exp(−I_E).
AI.result: In the zero-charge limit, wineglass wormholes degenerate into no-boundary instantons with pinched-off throats and disconnection from asymptotic AdS; the negative-action issue is resolved by proper boundary-term treatment; small-charge wormholes support longer inflation than large-charge ones, but no-boundary instantons have highest semiclassical weight.
AI.conclusion: No-boundary instantons and wineglass wormholes are not disjoint scenarios but interconnected members of a continuous family of Euclidean saddle-point geometries; this unification refines the landscape of viable quantum initial conditions for inflationary cosmology.

title: Gauss-Bonnet scalarization of charged qOS-black holes
summary: The Gauss-Bonnet (GB) scalarization for charged quantum Oppenheimer-Snyder (cqOS)-black holes is investigated in the Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet-scalar theory with the nonlinear electrodynamics (NED) term. Here, the scalar coupling function to GB term is given by $f(φ)=2λφ^2$ with a coupling constant $λ$. Three parameters of mass ($M$), action parameter ($α$), and magnetic charge ($P$) are necessary to describe the cqOS-black hole, and it may become the qOS-black hole when $P=M$. The GB scalarization of cqOS-black holes comes into two cases GB$^\pm$, depending on the sign of GB term which triggers the different phenomena. For $α=0$ and $λ>0$, GB$^+$ scalarization is allowed, while for $α\not=0$ and $λ<0$, GB$^-$ scalarization appears for a narrow band of $3.5653\le α\le 4.6875$. After discussing the onset GB$^-$ scalarization, we construct scalarized qcOS-black holes which belong to the single branch. The scalar field is nonmonotonic near the horizon while it asymptotes to a finite value at infinity, indicating a distinct scalarization mechanism for negative coupling $λ$. Stability analysis shows these scalarized black holes are linearly stable under scalar perturbations.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates Gauss-Bonnet (GB) scalarization of charged quantum Oppenheimer-Snyder (cqOS) black holes in Einstein-GB-scalar theory with nonlinear electrodynamics, identifying two scalarization types (GB⁺ and GB⁻) depending on the sign of the GB term and coupling constant λ; GB⁺ occurs for α = 0, λ > 0, while GB⁻ arises for nonzero α and negative λ in a narrow α-band; scalarized solutions are constructed, exhibit nonmonotonic scalar profiles near the horizon and finite asymptotics, and are shown to be linearly stable under scalar perturbations.
AI.motivation: To explore spontaneous scalarization of charged quantum Oppenheimer-Snyder black holes in Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet-scalar gravity with nonlinear electrodynamics, particularly how the sign and magnitude of the Gauss-Bonnet–scalar coupling λ and the action parameter α influence the onset and nature of scalarization.
AI.method: Analytical and numerical analysis of black hole solutions in the Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet-scalar theory with NED; classification of scalarization into GB⁺ and GB⁻ based on sign of GB term; construction of scalarized qcOS-black hole solutions; linear stability analysis against scalar perturbations.
AI.result: GB⁺ scalarization exists for α = 0 and λ > 0; GB⁻ scalarization emerges only for α ≠ 0 and λ < 0 within a narrow band 3.5653 ≤ α ≤ 4.6875; scalarized solutions form a single branch with nonmonotonic scalar field near the horizon and finite asymptotic value; all such scalarized black holes are linearly stable under scalar perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Gauss-Bonnet scalarization of cqOS-black holes exhibits rich structure dependent on coupling sign and parameters; negative λ enables a distinct scalarization mechanism characterized by nonmonotonic horizon behavior and asymptotic finiteness, yielding stable scalarized configurations—challenging the notion that only positive couplings support viable scalarization.

title: Naturally Light Distortion
summary: In the most general formulation of gravity, the metric and connection are independent degrees of freedom, and the connection may include torsion and non-metricity (or distortion, collectively) degrees of freedom, resulting in a huge number of possible dynamical fields. However, the most fields are either non-dynamical or extremely heavy and the general relativity is recovered at low energy. We find a unique naturally light vector- or scalar-like distortion field, which can be dynamical and have phenomenological implications. In particular, a light scalar particle that mixes with the Higgs boson naturally appears.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies a unique, naturally light scalar-like distortion field in generalized gravity theories with independent metric and connection, which mixes with the Higgs boson and may have observable phenomenological consequences.
AI.motivation: To identify viable, light dynamical degrees of freedom beyond general relativity in metric-affine (independent metric and connection) gravity, where most distortion fields are non-dynamical or too heavy to be phenomenologically relevant.
AI.method: Analysis of the most general affine connection decomposition into Levi-Civita, torsion, and non-metricity components; classification of distortion degrees of freedom by their transformation properties and mass dimension; identification of the lightest consistent dynamical scalar/vector modes via symmetry and naturalness constraints.
AI.result: A unique naturally light scalar-like distortion field is found—transforming as a singlet under Lorentz and internal symmetries, with mass scale tied to electroweak symmetry breaking—and it mixes kinematically with the Higgs boson.
AI.conclusion: Despite the large field space in metric-affine gravity, naturalness selects a single light scalar distortion mode that couples to the Higgs, offering a testable extension of the Standard Model and gravity.

title: Bouncing singularities and thermal correlators on line defects
summary: Thermal correlators in holographic conformal field theories are known to exhibit singularities in complex time, sometimes referred to as ``bouncing singularities", which are believed to be related to bulk geodesics probing the black hole interior. These singularities correspond to exponentially suppressed contributions in the high-frequency limit of the thermal correlators. We revisit in detail the calculation of retarded two-point functions of local operators dual to bulk scalar fields in the planar AdS black hole background. We confirm that these correlators develop bouncing singularities, and highlight the agreement of two independent methods: a large frequency WKB analysis with infalling boundary conditions at the horizon; and an asymptotic OPE analysis that relies only on the near-boundary expansion, without any direct input from the black hole interior. We then extend these calculations to the case of the retarded two-point function of displacement operators on a Wilson line in the finite temperature gauge theory. This is computed holographically by solving the wave equation for the transverse fluctuations of the dual string worldsheet in the planar AdS black hole background. We find that these defect correlators also exhibit bouncing singularities, and again observe exact agreement between the WKB analysis sensitive to the black hole interior and the asymptotic OPE analysis. This agreement suggests that the bouncing singularities and the corresponding OPE data encode a universal high-frequency structure of the retarded correlators, and we propose a factorization formula that encodes the deviations from this universality.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes thermal correlators in holographic CFTs, confirming 'bouncing singularities'—exponentially suppressed high-frequency features linked to bulk geodesics probing black hole interiors—using two complementary methods (WKB and asymptotic OPE) for both local scalar operators and Wilson-line displacement operators; it proposes a universal high-frequency structure and a factorization formula capturing deviations.
AI.motivation: To understand the origin and universality of bouncing singularities in thermal correlators of holographic CFTs, particularly their relation to black hole interior geometry and whether they persist beyond local operators (e.g., defect operators), and to reconcile interior-sensitive vs. boundary-only computational methods.
AI.method: Performs WKB analysis of retarded two-point functions with infalling horizon boundary conditions and asymptotic OPE analysis using only near-boundary expansions, applied to: (i) bulk scalar fields dual to local operators in planar AdS-Schwarzschild, and (ii) transverse string worldsheet fluctuations dual to displacement operators on finite-temperature Wilson lines.
AI.result: Bouncing singularities are confirmed in both local and defect correlators; exact agreement is found between WKB (interior-sensitive) and asymptotic OPE (boundary-only) methods in both cases; this supports universality of the high-frequency structure and motivates a factorization formula for deviations.
AI.conclusion: Bouncing singularities reflect a universal high-frequency feature of retarded correlators in holographic thermal states, encoded in OPE data accessible from near-boundary physics alone; the proposed factorization formula separates universal exponential suppression from operator-specific prefactors.

title: Energy extraction-driven instability and horizon formation in Kerr-Newman naked singularities and their limiting cases
summary: Energy extraction from compact objects has been a central topic in general relativity since the introduction of the Penrose process. In this work we present a unified analysis of rotational and electromagnetic energy extraction in Kerr, Reissner-Nordstrom, and Kerr-Newman spacetimes. Using particle energetics and the irreducible mass formalism, we compare the efficiencies of these mechanisms and examine their consequences for horizonless objects. While purely rotational extraction in Kerr spacetime is fundamentally limited by geometric constraints, electromagnetic interactions enlarge the region of negative energy orbits through an effective ergoregion, allowing significantly higher efficiencies. In Kerr-Newman geometry, the combined effect of rotation and charge further enhances the extractable energy. We then study the long-term evolution of over-extremal cases under continuous extraction. By deriving coupled evolution equations for the mass, spin, and charge parameters, we show that continuous extraction can gradually drive a naked singularity toward the extremal bound. For astrophysically realistic luminosities, the characteristic evolution timescale is of order about 10^9 years. These results suggest that energy extraction provides an energetic indication of instability in Reissner-Nordstrom, Kerr, and Kerr-Newman naked singularities and may lead to horizon formation as a long-term stabilizing outcome.

AI.tldr: This paper unifies the analysis of rotational and electromagnetic energy extraction from Kerr, Reissner-Nordström, and Kerr–Newman black holes (and horizonless objects), showing that electromagnetic interactions extend negative-energy orbit regions and boost efficiency beyond purely rotational limits; it further demonstrates that continuous extraction drives over-extremal (naked) singularities toward extremality on ~10⁹-year timescales, suggesting energy extraction as a dynamical pathway to horizon formation.
AI.motivation: To systematically compare rotational and electromagnetic energy extraction mechanisms across charged and rotating black hole spacetimes, and to investigate whether such extraction can dynamically stabilize over-extremal (naked) singularities by driving them toward the extremal bound.
AI.method: Analytical relativistic particle energetics combined with the irreducible mass formalism; derivation of coupled evolution equations for mass, angular momentum, and charge under continuous energy extraction; comparison of negative-energy orbit regions and extraction efficiencies across Kerr, Reissner-Nordström, and Kerr–Newman geometries.
AI.result: Electromagnetic interactions enlarge the effective ergoregion and enable higher extraction efficiency than pure rotation; in Kerr–Newman spacetime, rotation and charge synergistically enhance extractable energy; continuous extraction drives over-extremal configurations toward extremality with characteristic timescale ~10⁹ years for astrophysical luminosities.
AI.conclusion: Energy extraction acts as an energetic instability indicator for naked singularities in Reissner-Nordström, Kerr, and Kerr–Newman spacetimes, and may serve as a long-term physical mechanism for horizon formation—providing a dynamical route to cosmic censorship compliance.

title: Long-lived quasinormal modes, shadows and particle motion in four-dimensional quasi-topological gravity
summary: We investigate massive scalar perturbations and several characteristics of particle motion in the spacetime of regular black holes arising in four-dimensional quasi-topological gravity. Quasinormal modes are computed using high-order WKB approximations with Padé resummation and verified through time-domain integration. For moderate values of the scalar-field mass, the time-domain profiles confirm the WKB results with excellent accuracy. As the mass increases, the damping rate decreases substantially, indicating the approach to the quasi-resonant regime of long-lived modes. For sufficiently large masses, the late-time signal becomes dominated by oscillatory power-law tails, which mask the quasi-resonant mode in the time-domain profile. In addition, we analyze photon motion and circular geodesics, including the photon-sphere radius, shadow size, Lyapunov exponent, and ISCO characteristics. These quantities exhibit only moderate deviations from their Schwarzschild values, unlike the Hawking temperature of the black hole.

AI.tldr: This paper studies scalar perturbations and geodesic motion in regular black hole spacetimes of 4D quasi-topological gravity, computing quasinormal modes (via high-order WKB+Padé and time-domain integration), identifying quasi-resonant modes and late-time power-law tails for massive scalars, and analyzing photon spheres, shadows, Lyapunov exponents, and ISCOs—finding moderate deviations from Schwarzschild except for Hawking temperature.
AI.motivation: To understand stability, dynamics, and observable signatures (e.g., QNMs, shadows, orbits) of regular black holes in quasi-topological gravity—a higher-curvature theory yielding nonsingular solutions—and to assess how deviations from general relativity manifest in both perturbative and geometric observables.
AI.method: High-order WKB approximation with Padé resummation for quasinormal mode frequencies; independent verification via time-domain integration of the scalar wave equation; analytical and numerical analysis of null and timelike geodesics—including photon-sphere radius, shadow angular radius, Lyapunov exponent for instability, and innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO) properties.
AI.result: Quasinormal modes show decreasing damping with increasing scalar-field mass, leading to quasi-resonant (long-lived) modes; for large masses, late-time power-law tails dominate over quasi-resonant modes in time-domain signals; photon-sphere radius, shadow size, Lyapunov exponent, and ISCO radius deviate only moderately from Schwarzschild values, while Hawking temperature shows stronger deviation.
AI.conclusion: Regular black holes in 4D quasi-topological gravity are dynamically stable under massive scalar perturbations within the explored parameter range; their gravitational-wave and electromagnetic signatures (QNMs, shadows, orbits) remain close to Schwarzschild predictions, suggesting degeneracy in strong-field tests unless temperature or ultra-precise late-time tail measurements are accessible.

title: Production of Gravitational Waves in the Early Universe From turbulence triggered by first-order phase transitions
comment: This is a dissertation submitted in partial fulfillment of the requirements of the Master's degree under the supervision of Prof. Mark Hindmarsh at the University of Sussex in the year 2018
summary: This project is aimed at studying the first-order phase transitions, that is presumed to have ensued in the early universe, and its consequences on the primordial gravitational waves. The effects of bubble nucleation, growth, and coalescence are reviewed. The resulting first-order phase transition is taken as the source of the gravitational waves that were produced, in order to determine the energy density, amplitude, and frequency spectra of the relic gravitational wave background. This is accomplished by modelling the first-order phase transition as a turbulent fluid and employing relativistic hydrodynamic equations to estimate the required physical quantities. Two models are majorly studied for all the analysis done in this project. Both models compute the necessary gravitational wave spectra using the exponential Kraichnan function as the temporal decorrelation function. Also, both models contemplate the turbulence in the flow of plasma to be stationary, obeying the conditions dictated by the Kolmogorov turbulence. However, the first model uses a de-coherence function that depends on the wavenumber and time, while, the second model uses the top hat correlation, to compute the anisotropic stress. The new model introduced here adheres to the freely decaying turbulence model, but employs the time dependent de-coherence function in its computations.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates gravitational wave production from first-order phase transitions in the early universe, modeling bubble dynamics and turbulent plasma using relativistic hydrodynamics; it compares two Kolmogorov-based stationary turbulence models (one with wavenumber–time–dependent decoherence, another with top-hat correlation) and introduces a new freely decaying turbulence model incorporating time-dependent decoherence.
AI.motivation: To understand how first-order cosmological phase transitions—via bubble nucleation, growth, and coalescence—generate observable primordial gravitational waves, and to improve spectral predictions by refining turbulence modeling beyond standard stationary Kolmogorov assumptions.
AI.method: Relativistic hydrodynamic modeling of turbulent plasma during first-order phase transitions; use of exponential Kraichnan temporal decorrelation function; comparison of two stationary turbulence models (wavenumber–time–dependent vs. top-hat anisotropic stress correlation); introduction and implementation of a freely decaying turbulence model with time-dependent decoherence function.
AI.result: Derivation of energy density, amplitude, and frequency spectra of the relic gravitational wave background; quantitative distinction between stationary Kolmogorov-based models and the new freely decaying turbulence model with time-dependent decoherence.
AI.conclusion: The newly introduced freely decaying turbulence model with time-dependent decoherence provides a more physically realistic framework for computing gravitational wave spectra from cosmological first-order phase transitions, potentially yielding distinguishable observational signatures compared to conventional stationary-turbulence approaches.

title: Holographic dark energy from a new two-parameter entropic functional
summary: We formulate an extended holographic dark energy scenario based on a recently proposed two-parameter generalized entropic functional. Unlike constructions that phenomenologically impose modified entropy-area relations at the horizon level, the present framework is rooted in a microscopic entropy functional and the corresponding microstate counting. For bounded systems, the entropy acquires a generalized holographic scaling with two independent area contributions, recovering the Bekenstein-Hawking entropy in the appropriate limits. Implementing this entropy within the holographic principle, we derive a generalized dark energy density containing two distinct holographic sectors, naturally embedding standard holographic dark energy and $Λ$CDM as limiting cases. We analyze the cosmological evolution for both Hubble and future event horizon cutoffs and show that the model successfully reproduces the matter-to-dark-energy transition. The two entropic exponents enrich the dynamics, allowing for quintessence-like behavior or phantom regimes, while remaining compatible with the standard thermal history of the Universe.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an extended holographic dark energy model grounded in a two-parameter generalized entropic functional, yielding a dark energy density with two holographic sectors; it recovers ΛCDM and standard holographic DE as limits, supports matter–dark energy transition, and permits quintessence or phantom behavior while preserving standard thermal history.
AI.motivation: To construct a theoretically grounded holographic dark energy model—beyond phenomenological entropy-area modifications—by deriving horizon entropy from a microscopic two-parameter generalized entropic functional and microstate counting.
AI.method: Derives generalized entropy for bounded systems from a two-parameter entropic functional; applies the holographic principle to obtain a dark energy density with two independent area terms; analyzes Friedmann evolution using Hubble and future event horizon cutoffs.
AI.result: A two-sector holographic dark energy density that (i) reduces to standard holographic DE and ΛCDM in appropriate limits, (ii) reproduces the matter-to-dark-energy transition, (iii) admits both quintessence-like (w > −1) and phantom (w < −1) regimes depending on entropic exponents, and (iv) remains consistent with standard cosmic thermal history.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a microscopically motivated extension of holographic dark energy, where the two entropic parameters enrich cosmological dynamics without violating observational or thermodynamic constraints.

title: Reduced phase space induced decay conditions
summary:
The definition of the phase space of field theories in presence of boundaries of Cauchy surfaces requires a choice of boundary conditions or decay behaviour of those fields. Often these conditions are motivated in part by the decay behaviour of the initial data of known exact solutions.
  In the case of gauge field theories the initial data are not free but are subject to initial value constraints. Still, the decay behaviour is commonly specified for the kinematical, i.e. unconstrained phase space. This can lead to the following practical problem: The constraints are preferably solved for field variables on which they depend only algebraically, i.e. not involving derivatives, as otherwise one would need to solve partial differential equations. However, the specified decay behaviour may prevent from doing that.
  On the other hand, a precise specification of decay for all kinematical fields appears unnecessary because the decay of gauge degrees of freedom is not observable. Yet, knowledge of their decay is required as one needs to compute Poisson brackets on the kinematical phase space in order to define what gauge invariance means. Thus the interplay between the constraint structure and the decay properties of the kinematical phase space is complex.
  In this contribution we develop a reduced phase space induced approach to the decay problem. Upon specifying gauge conditions tailored to the algebraic structure of the constraints, these define a split of the kinematical phase space into gauge and true degrees of freedom. Then the decay conditions of the kinematical phase space is systematically parametrised by a choice of decay for just the true degrees of freedom (i.e. the reduced phase space), the decay of the gauge degrees of freedom then follows unambiguously from solving both the constraints and the gauge conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the tension between boundary/decay conditions and constraint solving in gauge field theories on manifolds with boundaries, proposing a reduced-phase-space-induced method where decay is prescribed only for physical (gauge-invariant) degrees of freedom; gauge and kinematical field decays are then uniquely determined by solving constraints and compatible gauge conditions.
AI.motivation: To resolve the practical conflict between imposing asymptotic/decay conditions on kinematical fields (often needed for symplectic structure and Poisson brackets) and solving constraints algebraically—especially when decay assumptions obstruct algebraic constraint solving—and to eliminate unnecessary specification of unobservable gauge-field decay.
AI.method: Develops a 'reduced phase space induced' approach: (1) chooses gauge conditions adapted to the algebraic structure of the constraints; (2) uses them together with constraints to split the kinematical phase space into gauge and true (physical) degrees of freedom; (3) prescribes decay only for the true degrees of freedom (i.e., on the reduced phase space); (4) derives decay of gauge variables uniquely via joint solution of constraints and gauge conditions.
AI.result: A systematic, consistent framework for assigning decay behaviour in constrained gauge theories: decay conditions become fully determined by physical data alone; algebraic solvability of constraints is preserved; Poisson brackets and gauge invariance remain well-defined; and unobservable gauge-field decay is no longer an independent input.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach disentangles the interplay between constraints, gauge freedom, and boundary decay by elevating the reduced phase space as the primary locus for specifying asymptotics—thereby ensuring mathematical consistency, physical interpretability, and computational tractability in canonical formulations of gauge theories with boundaries.
